(Regarding the subjugation of the sun and the moon, we had a detailed discussion in Volume 10 under verse 2 of Surah Ar-Ra'd and verse 33 of Ibrahim—pages 120 and 354).

[^1]: Regarding the gradual change of night and day, we discussed this in Volume 2, page 367, under verse 27 of Surah Al-Imran.

However, while this sun and moon revolve in their paths with perfect regularity and are worthy and good servants for humanity, the system governing them is not eternal. Even these massive planets, with all their light and brilliance, will eventually become dark and cease to function. Therefore, following the discussion of subjugation, He adds: "each running its course for a specified term" (كُلٌّ يَجْرِي لِأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى).

And according to the verses: “When the sun is wrapped up [in darkness], and when the stars fall, losing their lustre” (Surah At-Takwir, verses 1 and 2), they will all eventually head toward darkness and extinction.

Some commentators have provided another interpretation for "specified term" (Ajal Musamma) here, which is the orbital motion of the sun and the moon—the former completing its cycle in one full year and the latter in one month. [^1] However, considering the usage of this expression in numerous verses of the Holy Quran where it means the end of life, it becomes clear that the aforementioned interpretation is incorrect; rather, it is the first interpretation, meaning the end of the lifespan of the sun and the moon (refer to verses 61 of An-Nahl, 45 of Fatir, 42 of Az-Zumar, 4 of An-Nur, and 67 of Ghafir).

Then, as a conclusion to this monotheistic discussion, He says: "That is Allah, your Lord" (ذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ).

The God who has ordained the system of light and darkness and the calculated movements of the sun and the moon with all their blessings.

"To Him belongs all sovereignty" (له الملك).

"And those you invoke besides Him do not possess even as much as the thin skin of a date stone" (وَ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ ما يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْ قِطْمِيرٍ). [^1] According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, "Qitmir" is the mark found on the back of a date stone (a small depression). According to Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and Qurtubi in his Tafsir, it is the thin white skin that covers the entire stone. In any case, it is a metaphor for extremely small and insignificant things.

Yes, these idols are neither the source of benefit nor the cause of harm; they neither defend you nor themselves; they possess neither sovereignty nor ownership, not even over the skin of a date stone! Despite this, how do you, the unwise, worship them and ask them to solve your problems?!

[^1]: The use of "Alladhina" (those), which is usually for a plural of rational masculine beings, regarding idols is due to the illusion the pagans had about these inanimate objects; the Quran mentions their own expression and then severely rejects it.

Then He adds: "If you invoke them, they do not hear your call" (إِنْ تَدْعُوهُمْ لا يَسْمَعُوا دُعاءَكُمْ). For they are nothing more than pieces of stone and wood—inanimate and devoid of intellect! "And even if they heard, they could not respond to you" (وَ لَوْ سَمِعُوا مَا اسْتَجابُوا لَكُمْ).

Because it has been clarified that they do not own benefit or harm in the universe even to the extent of the thin skin of a date stone; given this, how do you expect them to do something for you or untie a knot?

And even higher than this: "And on the Day of Resurrection, they will disown your polytheism" (وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ).

And they will say: "O Lord, these people were not worshipping us; rather, they were in truth worshipping their own base desires."

This testimony and witness is either through their "state of being" (Lisan al-Hal)—such that anyone who looks at the idols hears this speech from them with the ear of intellect—or that the same God who will make the limbs, organs, and skin of humans speak on that day will command them to speak, so they may testify that these deviant idolaters were in reality worshipping their own delusions and lusts.

This verse is similar to what appears in verse 28 of Surah Yunus, where He says: “And [mention] the Day We will gather them all together; then We will say to those who associated others with Allah, '[Remain in] your place, you and your "partners."' Then We will separate them, and their "partners" will say, 'You did not used to worship us'”.

A group of commentators have suggested the possibility that these expressions refer to deities such as the angels and Prophet Jesus عليه السلام, because only they are capable of speaking on the Day of Resurrection, and the phrase "If you invoke them, they do not hear your call" refers to the fact that they are so occupied with themselves that if you call them, they do not hear your words. [^1] However, considering the breadth of the concept "And those you invoke besides Him," it appears that the intended meaning is the idols, especially since the phrase "If you invoke them, they do not hear your call" apparently pertains to this world.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis, He says: "And none can inform you like the One who is All-Aware" (وَ لا يُنَبِّئُكَ مِثْلُ خَبِيرٍ).

If He says that the idols will deny your worship on the Day of Resurrection and disassociate themselves from you, do not be surprised, for the One informing of this is He who is aware of the entire universe and every single atom within it; the future is as clear and manifest before His knowledge as the past and the present.

Although the addressee in this sentence is apparently the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is evident that it refers to all human beings.

[^1]: This possibility is mentioned in the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan, Al-Alusi, and Qurtubi.

Key Points The Misuse of Verses and Deviant Interpretations

Although it became clear during the commentary of the verses that the last verse under discussion ("If you invoke them, they do not hear your call...") refers to idols—which neither have ears to hear the requests of their worshippers, nor would they be able to solve any problem if they did, nor do they possess an atom's weight of ownership or sovereignty in the universe—a group of superficial Wahhabis have clung to this verse and its like to sever the connection of Muslims with the Prophet of Islam and the great leaders through seeking intercession (Tawassul) and asking for shafa'ah. They claim the Quran says: "All those whom you call besides God—even the prophets!—do not hear your speech, and if they heard, they would not respond."

Or as stated in verse 197 of Surah Al-A'raf: "And those you invoke besides Him are unable to help you, nor can they help themselves."

In this way, they negate any form of Tawassul to the souls of the prophets and Imams and consider it contrary to Monotheism (Tawhid)! This is while a simple look at the verses before and after these is sufficient to understand the reality that they refer to idols. In all of them, the discussion is about idols—about stones and wood that they imagined to be partners with God and for which they believed in a power independent of God's power.

But who is it that does not know that the prophets and the Awliya (saints), like the martyrs in the way of God—whose life the Quran explicitly speaks of—possess a purgatorial life (Barzakh)? And we know that in the purgatorial life, the activity of the soul is more expansive and wider, because it has been freed from material veils and worldly attachments. This is from one perspective.

From another perspective, undoubtedly, Tawassul to these pure souls does not mean that we grant them independence against Allah; rather, the goal is to seek help from their honor and status before God and to ask for assistance through the respect and greatness they hold in the presence of God, which is the essence of Monotheism and servitude to the Lord (reflect carefully).

Therefore, just as the Quran explicitly says regarding the issue of intercession that they intercede by the permission and command of God: "Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (Al-Baqarah: 255), similarly, Tawassul to them is also through this same path and channel.

Who can deny the explicit verses of Tawassul? Or consider it polytheism and stand against the Quran while claiming Monotheism? None but the arrogant ignorant ones who have raised these ominous tunes that lead to the creation of division among Muslims!

Thus, we read in the accounts of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that in times of difficulty, they would come to the grave of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, seek Tawassul, and ask for help from his pure soul in the presence of God.

As Al-Bayhaqi, the famous Sunni traditionist, narrates: during the time of the second Caliph, there was a drought and famine. Bilal, accompanied by a group of companions, came to the grave of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Messenger of Allah, ask for rain for your Ummah... for they have perished..." [^1] Some Sunni commentators like Al-Alusi have narrated many hadiths in this regard, and after discussion and strictness regarding these hadiths, he finally says: "After all this discussion, I see no obstacle in Tawassul to the presence of God through the status of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, whether during his life or after his death..." Then he adds others who have a status before God and admits the permissibility of Tawassul to them. [^2] We also had another detailed discussion on this subject in Volume 4, page 366, under verse 35 of Surah Al-Ma'idah.

[^1]: From the book Al-Tawassul ila Haqiqat al-Tawassul. [^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani.

Ayahs

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ أَنتُمُ ٱلْفُقَرَآءُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (١٥)
O mankind! You are the ones who stand in need of Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (15)
إِن يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (١٦)
If He wishes, He will take you away, and bring about a new creation; (16)
وَمَا ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ بِعَزِيزٍ (١٧)
and that is not a hard thing for Allah. (17)
وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ وَإِن تَدْعُ مُثْقَلَةٌ إِلَىٰ حِمْلِهَا لَا يُحْمَلْ مِنْهُ شَىْءٌ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰٓ ۗ إِنَّمَا تُنذِرُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۚ وَمَن تَزَكَّىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَتَزَكَّىٰ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٨)
No bearer shall bear another’s burden, and should someone heavily burdened call [another] to carry it, nothing of it will be carried [by anyone] even if he should be a near relative. You can only warn those who fear their Lord in secret and maintain the prayer. Whoever purifies himself, purifies only for his own sake, and to Allah is the return. (18)

Commentary

No One Shall Bear the Burden of Another

Following the emphatic call toward monotheism and the struggle against all forms of polytheism and idol worship mentioned in the previous verses, a misconception might arise for some: What need does Allah have for our worship that He insists and emphasizes it so much? Therefore, in the verses under discussion, to clarify the reality that we are in need of His worship, not He in need of ours, He says: "O mankind! You are the ones in need of Allah, and Allah is the Self-Sufficient, the Praiseworthy" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنْتُمُ الْفُقَرَاءُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَاللَّهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ).

What a profound and valuable statement that clarifies our position in the arena of existence before the Giver of Existence, unlocks many enigmas, and answers numerous questions.

Indeed, the truly Self-Sufficient and Self-Subsisting Being in the entire universe is only One, and that is Allah. All human beings—indeed, all creatures—are entirely in need, impoverished, and dependent on that Independent Being; if their connection were severed for a single moment, they would be nothing and void.

Just as He is Absolute Sufficiency, humans are absolute poverty; and just as He is Self-Subsisting, all creatures subsist through Him. For He is an infinite Being in every respect, and the Necessary Existence (Wajib al-Wujud) in His Essence and Attributes.

That being the case, what need could He possibly have for our worship? It is we who tread the path of perfection through His worship and obedience, and in the light of servitude to Him, we draw closer moment by moment to that infinite Source of Grace and benefit from the lights of His Essence and Attributes.

In reality, this verse is an explanation for the preceding verses which stated: "That is Allah, your Lord; to Him belongs the sovereignty..." (ذلِكَكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَهُ الْمُلْكُ). This is Allah, your Lord, to whom ownership and governance in the universe exclusively belong, while others possess nothing of their own, not even as much as the thin skin of a date stone.

Therefore, humans are in need of Him and not anyone else; thus, they should never bow their heads in submission at the threshold of anyone but Him, nor seek the fulfillment of their needs from others, for they too are all needy and impoverished like themselves. Even the honoring of the Divine Prophets and the leaders of truth is because they are His messengers and representatives, not because they possess independence of their own.

Consequently, He is both "Ghani" (Self-Sufficient) and "Hamid" (Praiseworthy). That is, while being Self-Sufficient, He is so Generous and Merciful that He is worthy of all praise and gratitude; and while being Generous and Kind to His servants, He remains independent of everyone.

Attending to this reality produces two positive effects in believers: on one hand, it brings them down from the mount of pride, selfishness, and rebellion, warning them that they possess nothing of their own to boast about—whatever exists is a trust from the Lord in their possession.

On the other hand, they will not stretch out the hand of need toward anyone but Him, nor place the collar of servitude to other than Allah around their necks; they will be free from the color of all attachments so that everyone becomes a servant to their high ambition.

With this vision and worldview, believers see whatever exists in the world as a reflection of His existence, and focusing on the "means" never makes them heedless of the "Causer of Causes" (Musabbib al-Asbab).

A group of philosophers have considered this verse an allusion to the famous argument of "Poverty and Contingency" (Faqr wa Imkan) or "Contingency and Necessity" regarding the proof of the Necessary Existence. Although the verse is not in the position of presenting a formal argument to prove God's existence but rather recounts His attributes, the aforementioned argument can be considered a necessary implication of the verse's content.

Explanation of the Argument of Contingency and Necessity (Poverty and Sufficiency)

All the beings we see in this world were once non-existent and then donned the raiment of existence, or more precisely, they were once nothing and then became existence. This is proof that they are the "effect" of another existence and do not possess being from themselves.

We know that every "effected" existence is dependent upon and subsists through its "cause" and is entirely in need and requirement. If that cause is also the effect of another cause, it too will be needy and dependent in its turn. If this chain continues in an infinite regress, we would have a collection of needy and impoverished beings. It is certain that such a collection would never come into existence, because infinite need is still need, and infinite poverty is still poverty; a number never arises from infinite zeros, and independence is never achieved from infinite dependencies.

From this, we conclude that we must ultimately reach a Being who is Self-Subsisting and independent in all respects, who is a Cause and not an effect, and He is the Necessary Existence (Wajib al-Wujud). ^1

^1: It should be noted that the argument of contingency and necessity has two interpretations because philosophers have defined "contingency" (imkan) in two ways: quidditative contingency and existential contingency. Since the view of investigative philosophers is based on the "primacy of existence" (asalat al-wujud), contingency here must be interpreted as existential contingency, meaning the need and dependence on a cause in the very essence of existence (read more on this in philosophical texts).

Here, the question arises: Why does the above verse only speak of humans and their need for Allah, while this poverty is a universal aspect of the entire universe?

The answer is that if man, who is the "crown of creation" in this world, is entirely in need of Him, then the state of the rest of the creatures is clear. In other words, others share with man the cause of poverty, which is the contingency of existence.

The mention of man specifically is because the goal was to bring man down from the mount of pride and to draw his attention to his need for God in all states, in everything, and everywhere. This very attention is the primary root of virtuous traits and moral habits; it is the secret of humility, the abandonment of oppression and tyranny, the abandonment of pride, arrogance, and vanity, and the abandonment of stinginess, greed, and envy; it is the motivation for humility before the Truth.

In the following verse, to emphasize this very poverty and need of humans toward Him, He says: "If He wills, He can take you away and bring a new creation" (إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ وَيَأْتِ بِخَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ).

Therefore, He has no need for you or your worship; it is you who are in need of Him.

This verse is similar to what appears in Surah Al-An'am, where it says: "And your Lord is the Self-Sufficient, the Possessor of Mercy. If He wills, He can take you away and cause whom He wills to succeed you after you, just as He produced you from the descendants of another people" (Al-An'am: 133).

He neither needs your obedience nor fears your sins, and yet His vast mercy encompasses you all. Neither the vanishing of the entire world diminishes anything from His greatness, nor does the creation of this universe add anything to His station of Majesty.

And in the final verse, again as a further emphasis, He says: "And that is not difficult for Allah" (وَمَا ذلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ بِعَزِيزٍ).

Indeed, whatever He wills, He commands it "Be," and it immediately becomes. Let alone man, this statement holds true for the entire universe.

In any case, if He commands you to have faith, obedience, and worship, it is all for your own sake, and its benefits and blessings return to you.

The last verse under discussion points to five "Key Points" in connection with the previous verses:

First, the previous verses stated that "If Allah wills, He can take you away and bring another people in your place." This statement might raise a question for some: the audience of this verse are not all sinners, for in every age and time, there have been and are righteous believers; is it possible that they too would suffer the consequences of others' sins and be doomed to annihilation?

It is here that He says: "And no bearer of burdens shall bear the burden of another" (وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ).

"Wizr" means heavy, and it is derived from "wazar" (rhyming with nazar), which means a mountain refuge. Sometimes it also means responsibility, just as a "Wazir" (minister) is called so because he bears a heavy burden of responsibility; "muwazarah" also means assistance because everyone, when assisting, bears a portion of another's burden.

This sentence, which is one of the fundamental pillars of Islamic beliefs, relates on one hand to the Justice of God, who considers everyone accountable for their own deeds, rewarding their efforts and punishing their sins.

On the other hand, it points to the severity of the punishment on the Day of Resurrection, where no one will be willing to bear the burden of another's sin, no matter how much they may love them.

Attention to this meaning has a great impact on human self-building: that everyone should watch over themselves and never surrender to corruption under the pretext that those around them or their society is corrupt, nor consider the pollution of the environment as a justification for their own pollution, for everyone bears the burden of their own sin.

Furthermore, it gives humans the insight that Allah's accounting with societies is not a collective and unified accounting; rather, everyone is held accountable independently. That is, if one has fulfilled their duty regarding both self-purification and the struggle against corruption, there is no fear or dread for them, even if the entire world besides them were polluted with disbelief, polytheism, oppression, and sin.

Fundamentally, no educational program will be effective without drawing attention to this basic principle (reflect on this).

In the second sentence, He presents the same issue in another way, saying: "And if a 'heavily laden' soul calls another to carry its load, nothing of it will be carried, even if he be a near relative" (وَإِنْ تَدْعُ مُثْقَلَةٌ إِلَىٰ حِمْلِهَا لَا يُحْمَلْ مِنْهُ شَيْءٌ وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ). ^1

In a tradition, it is mentioned that on the Day of Judgment, a mother and child are brought, both bearing a heavy burden of sin. The mother pleads with the child: "In exchange for all those hardships I endured for you in the world, carry some of the burden of my sin." The child says to the mother: "Get away from me, for I am more troubled than you!" ^1

Here the question arises: Does this verse contradict what has been mentioned in numerous narrations regarding "good" and "evil" traditions (sunnah)? For these narrations say: "Whoever establishes a good tradition, the reward of all those who act upon it will be written for him without their reward being diminished; and whoever establishes an evil tradition, the burden of those who act upon it will be upon him without their sin being diminished."

However, with attention to one point, the answer to this question becomes clear: a person is not charged with another's sin if they had no involvement in it. But if they had a share in creating that act through establishing a tradition, or assistance and help, or encouragement and persuasion, it is certainly considered their own deed, and they are a partner and participant in it.

Finally, in the third sentence, He unveils the reality that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) warnings only affect ready hearts, saying: "You only warn those who fear their Lord in the unseen and establish prayer" (إِنَّمَا تُنْذِرُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ وَأَقَامُوا الصَّلَاةَ).

^1: Although this tradition is sometimes narrated in various commentaries from Fudayl ibn Iyad and sometimes from Ibn Abbas, it seems unlikely they said this of their own accord; it is possible the original tradition is from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) (refer to the Tafsirs of Abu al-Futuh, Qurtubi, and Ruh al-Bayan).

Until there is fear of God in a heart, and until one feels the surveillance of a spiritual power over themselves in secret and in public, and unless one aids this inner feeling by performing prayer—which enlivens the heart and keeps it in remembrance of God—the warnings of the Prophets and Saints will be ineffective.

In the beginning, when a person has not yet chosen any belief or embraced faith, if they do not possess a spirit of truth-seeking and do not feel responsibility toward recognizing realities, they will not listen to the call of the Prophets, nor will they reflect upon the signs of the Lord in the universe!

In the fourth sentence, He returns to the reality that God is independent of everyone and adds: "And whoever purifies himself, he only purifies himself for his own soul" (وَمَنْ تَزَكَّىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَتَزَكَّىٰ لِنَفْسِهِ).

And finally, in the fifth and last sentence, He warns that if the good and the bad do not reach the results of their deeds in this world, it does not matter, for "to Allah is the final return," and He will ultimately settle the account of all! (وَإِلَى اللَّهِ الْمَصِيرُ).

Ayahs

وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ وَٱلْبَصِيرُ (١٩)
The blind one and the seer are not equal, (19)
وَلَا ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتُ وَلَا ٱلنُّورُ (٢٠)
nor darkness and light; (20)
وَلَا ٱلظِّلُّ وَلَا ٱلْحَرُورُ (٢١)
nor shade and torrid heat; (21)
وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْأَحْيَآءُ وَلَا ٱلْأَمْوَٰتُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسْمِعُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ بِمُسْمِعٍ مَّن فِى ٱلْقُبُورِ (٢٢)
nor are the living equal to the dead. Indeed Allah makes whomever He wishes to hear, and you cannot make those who are in the graves hear you. (22)
إِنْ أَنتَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ (٢٣)
You are but a warner. (23)

Commentary

Light and Darkness Are Not Equal!

In connection with the discussions regarding faith and disbelief in the preceding verses, the verses under discussion present four striking parables for the believer and the disbeliever, in which the effects of "faith" and "disbelief" are manifested in the clearest possible way:

In the first parable, the "disbeliever" and the "believer" are likened to the "blind" and the "seeing," stating: "The blind and the seeing are not equal" (وَمَا يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمَىٰ وَالْبَصِيرُ).

Faith is light and illuminating; it grants man radiance and awareness in his worldview, creed, action, and entire life. Disbelief, however, is darkness and obscurity; within it, there is neither a correct insight into the universe nor any sign of sound belief or righteous action.

The Holy Quran, in verse 257 of Surah Al-Baqarah, aptly conveys this point:

اللَّهُ وَلِيُّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَوْلِياؤُهُمُ الطَّاغُوتُ يُخْرِجُونَهُمْ مِنَ النُّورِ إِلَى الظُّلُماتِ أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ "Allah is the Protector of those who believe. He brings them out from darknesses into the light. And those who disbelieve—their protectors are the Taghut; they take them out of the light into darknesses. Those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally!"

And since a seeing eye alone is not enough—there must also be illumination and light so that man may observe beings with the help of these two factors—the next verse adds: "Nor are the darknesses and the light [equal]" (وَلَا الظُّلُمَاتُ وَلَا النُّورُ).

For darkness is the source of straying; darkness is the cause of stagnation and inertia; darkness is the cause of all kinds of dangers. But light and radiance are the source of life, vitality, movement, stir, growth, development, and perfection. If light were to vanish, all energies in the world would be extinguished, and death would encompass the entire material realm. Such is the light of faith in the spiritual world: the factor of growth and perfection and the cause of life and movement.

Then it adds: "Nor are the (soothing) shade and the scorching heat equal" (وَلَا الظِّلُّ وَلَا الْحَرُورُ).

The believer lives in tranquility, peace, and security under the shade of his faith, while the disbeliever burns in distress and suffering due to his disbelief.

Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Harur" (on the weight of qabul) means a hot and scorching wind (a deadly and drying wind).

Some have interpreted it as the Samum wind (poisonous wind), and others as the intensity of the sun's heat.

Zamakhshari says in Al-Kashshaf: "Samum" refers to the harmful and lethal winds that blow during the day, but "Harur" refers to these same winds whether they occur during the day or at night. In any case, how can such a wind be compared to the cool and refreshing shade that caresses the human soul and body?

Finally, in the last comparison, it says: "And the living and the dead are not equal!" (وَمَا يَسْتَوِي الْأَحْيَاءُ وَلَا الْأَمْوَاتُ). Believers are the living; they possess effort, endeavor, movement, and vitality. They have growth, development, branches, leaves, flowers, and fruit. But the disbeliever is like a dried piece of wood that has "neither freshness, nor leaves, nor flowers, nor shade," and is useful for nothing but burning.

In verse 122 of Surah Al-An'am, we read: أَوَمَنْ كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِنْهَا "Is he who was dead and We gave him life and made for him a light by which he walks among the people like one who is in darknesses, never to emerge therefrom?"

And at the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed, Allah makes whom He wills to hear" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُسْمِعُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ), so that they may hear the call of Truth with the ear of the soul and respond to the cry of the heralds of Monotheism.

"And you cannot make those who are in the graves hear!" (وَمَا أَنْتَ بِمُسْمِعٍ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ). No matter how resonant your cry, how heart-touching your words, and how eloquent your expression, the dead perceive nothing from it. Those who have lost their human spirit due to persistence in sin and immersion in prejudice, obstinacy, injustice, and corruption are certainly not prepared to accept your call.

Therefore, do not be grieved by their lack of faith, and do not be restless; your duty is only to convey and warn: "You are not but a warner" (إِنْ أَنْتَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ).

Key Points

1- The Effects of Faith and Disbelief

We know that the Quran does not grant importance to geographical, racial, or class boundaries and the like that separate humans from one another. It considers the only boundary to be that of "faith" and "disbelief," and thus divides the entire human society into two groups: "believer" and "disbeliever."

In introducing faith, the Quran frequently likens it to "light" and disbelief to "darkness." This comparison is the most vivid representation of the Quran's conception of disbelief and faith. [^1] Faith is a type of inner perception and vision, a kind of knowledge and awareness coupled with heart-felt conviction and movement—a belief that penetrates the depths of the human soul and becomes the source of constructive activities.

Disbelief, however, is ignorance, unawareness, and lack of belief, the result of which is a lack of motivation, a loss of the sense of responsibility, and satanic and destructive movements.

We also know that "light" in the material world is the origin of every kind of life, movement, growth, and development in humans, animals, and plants. Conversely, darkness is the factor of extinction and sleep, and if it persists, it leads to death and the destruction of life.

Therefore, it is not surprising that in the above verses, "faith" and "disbelief" are likened in one place to "light" and "darkness," in another to "life" and "death," in one place to soothing shade and the Samum wind, and in another, the "believer" and "disbeliever" are likened to the "seeing" and the "blind."

All that needs to be said is expressed within these four comparisons.

We need not look far; when we sit and interact with a "believer," we feel the effect of this light in their entire being. Their thoughts are illuminating, their words are radiant, and their actions and character acquaint us with the reality of life and true existence.

But from the "disbeliever," darkness pours forth from their entire being. They think of nothing but their material and fleeting interests; the horizon of their thought does not exceed the limits of their personal life; they are immersed in lusts, and their companionship plunges the human heart and soul into waves of darkness: The company of the dead brings about death, The conversation of the cold-hearted brings about coldness!

Thus, what the Quran has expressed in these verses is perceivable in a tangible and sensible manner.

2- Do the Dead Not Perceive Any Reality?

Given what has been stated in the above verses, two questions arise:

First: How can the Quran say, "You cannot make your voice reach the dead," while it is mentioned in a famous Hadith that on the day of the Battle of Badr, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the bodies of the disbelievers to be thrown into a well after the battle ended, then he called out to them and said: "Have you found what Allah and His Messenger promised to be true? For I have found what Allah promised me to be true!"

Here, Umar objected and said, "O Messenger of Allah! How do you speak to bodies in which there is no soul?!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "You do not hear what I say better than they do, except that they are unable to respond!" [^1] Or the fact that one of the rites for the deceased is to have the true beliefs dictated (talqin) to them—how is this consistent with the verses under discussion?

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: the verses under discussion speak of the dead's lack of perception in an ordinary and natural sense. However, the narration of the Battle of Badr or the talqin of the deceased pertains to extraordinary conditions where Allah makes the words of His Prophet reach those dead in an extraordinary manner.

In other words, the connection of a human in the world of Barzakh (the Isthmus) with the world of this life is severed, except in cases where Allah commands this connection to be established. For this reason, under normal circumstances, we cannot establish a connection with the dead.

The second question: If our voice does not reach the dead, what is the meaning of sending salutations (Salam) upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams عليه السلام, seeking their intercession (Tawassul), visiting their graves, and requesting their intercession before Allah?

A group of Wahhabis, generally known for their intellectual rigidity, relying on this same notion from initial outward appearances without examining other verses of the Quran or giving weight to the many Hadiths narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, have denied the issue of Tawassul and, in their own imagination, struck a line of invalidity through it.

The answer to this question also becomes clear from what we said in response to the first question. The account of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the men of God is separate from others; they are like the "martyrs" (rather, in the front rank before them) and are eternally living. According to the verse "They are alive with their Lord, receiving provision," they benefit from the provisions of their Lord and, by Allah's command, maintain their connection with this world, just as in this world they could establish a connection with the dead—such as those killed at Badr.

For this reason, in numerous narrations found in both Sunni and Shi'a books, we read: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams عليه السلام hear the words of those who send salutations upon them from near and far, and they respond to them, and even the deeds of the Ummah are presented to them. [^1] It is noteworthy that we are commanded to send salutations upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the Tashahhud of the prayer, and this is the belief of all Muslims, both Shi'a and Sunni. How could it be possible to address him with something he never hears?!

In several narrations in Sahih Muslim from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri and Abu Hurairah, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself that he said: "Dictate (laqqinu) to your dead 'There is no god but Allah'." [^2] In Nahjul Balagha, the issue of establishing a connection with the souls of the dead is also mentioned, where Ali عليه السلام spoke with the souls of the believers who were in the cemetery behind Kufa. [^3]

3- Diversity of Expressions is Part of Eloquence

In the four comparisons mentioned in the above verses, completely different expressions are seen. For example, "blind" (a'ma), "seeing" (basir), "shade" (zill), and "heat" (harur) appear in the singular form, while "living" (ahya) and "dead" (amwat) are both in the plural. "Darknesses" (zulumat) and "light" (nur) appear with one in the plural and the other in the singular.

On the other hand, in the first and second comparisons, that which has a negative aspect is mentioned first (the blind and darknesses), while in the third and fourth comparisons, that which has a positive aspect takes precedence (shade and the living).

Thirdly, in the first comparison, the particle of negation is not repeated, while in the other three comparisons, it is repeated.

Fourthly, the phrase "are not equal" (ma yastawi) appears only in the first and last comparisons and is absent in the others.

Some commentators have mentioned points for these differences, some of which are noteworthy and others subject to critique.

Among the noteworthy points is that "darknesses" is plural and "light" is singular because darkness—meaning disbelief—has many branches, but the truth of faith and Monotheism is but one. Faith is like a straight line, and between two points, there is only one straight line; but the darkness of disbelief is like deviant lines, and between two points, thousands upon thousands of deviant lines exist.

Also, the precedence of negative aspects in the first two examples refers to the beginning of Islam, when people were guided from the blindness of the Age of Ignorance and the darknesses of polytheism to the light and vision of Islam. The other two examples refer to other stages when Islam had strengthened its roots in the soil of hearts and spread its positive aspects throughout society.

Beyond these, diversity in expression fundamentally gives a special spirit and freshness to speech, making it pleasant, beautiful, and interesting. In contrast, monotonous repetition—except in exceptional cases—takes away the grace of speech. For this reason, eloquent and articulate speakers always strive to make the expressions of their discourse diverse and pleasant, and we know that the Quran is at the highest degree of eloquence and clarity.

Therefore, if there were no point other than the observance of eloquence, this meaning would suffice, although future generations may discover other secrets in these expressions beyond what we have mentioned that are hidden from us today.

[^1]: Refer to verses: 257-Baqarah, 15-Ma'idah, 16-Ma'idah, 1-Ibrahim, 5-Ibrahim, 22-Zumar, 11-Hadid, 12-Talaq. [^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan under the verses discussed; this same Hadith appears in Sahih Bukhari with slight differences (Sahih Bukhari, Vol. 5, p. 97, Chapter on the Killing of Abu Jahl). [^1]: Kashf al-Irtiyab, p. 109. Under verse 105 of Surah At-Tawbah, we also referred to the narrations of the "presentation of deeds" (Refer to Vol. 8 of Tafsir Nemouneh, p. 125). [^2]: Sahih Muslim, Book of Funerals, Hadith 1 and 2 (Vol. 2, p. 631). [^3]: Nahjul Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 130.

Ayahs

إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا ۚ وَإِن مِّنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلَا فِيهَا نَذِيرٌ (٢٤)
Indeed We have sent you with the truth as a bearer of good news and as a warner; and there is not a nation but a warner has passed in it. (24)
وَإِن يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَبِٱلزُّبُرِ وَبِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُنِيرِ (٢٥)
If they impugn you, those before them have impugned [likewise]: their apostles brought them manifest proofs, [holy] writs, and illuminating scriptures. (25)
ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ (٢٦)
Then I seized the faithless. So how was My rebuttal! (26)

Commentary

It is No Wonder if the Blind-Hearted Do Not Believe

In the preceding verses, we reached the point where there are individuals like the dead and the blind, upon whose hearts the words of the prophets have the least effect. Following that, in the verses under discussion, to comfort the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in this regard so that he does not become overly grieved and distressed, He first says: "Indeed, We have sent you with the truth as a bringer of glad tidings and a warner, and there was no nation in the past but that there had passed within it a warner" (إِنَّا أَرْسَلْناكَ بِالْحَقِّ بَشِيراً وَ نَذِيراً وَ إِنْ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلا فِيها نَذِيرٌ).

It is sufficient for you that you do not fall short in performing the duty of "bringing glad tidings" and "warning." Convey your call to their ears, give glad tidings of divine rewards, and warn them of the Lord's punishments, whether they accept or persist in their enmity and stubbornness.

It is noteworthy that in the last verse of the previous discussion, He said: "You are but a warner" (إِنْ أَنْتَ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ), but in the first verse under discussion, He says: "We have sent you as a bringer of glad tidings and a warner with the truth," indicating that if you are a warner, you do not do this of your own accord; it is a mission that We have placed upon you.

And if the previous verse relied solely on "warning," it was because the discussion concerned stubborn ignorant people who, like the dead in a graveyard, were unreceptive to any word. However, here He describes the duty of the prophets in general, which encompasses both aspects of glad tidings and warning. Nevertheless, at the end of this verse, He again emphasizes the "warner" because the primary part of the prophets' call against polytheists and oppressors has been through "warning."

"Khala" (خلا) is derived from the root "khala’" (خلاء), which originally means a place where there is no covering. This word is used for both time and place. Since time passes, past eras are called "azmanah khaliyah" (past/empty eras), because no trace of them remains now, and the world has become empty of them.

Therefore, the sentence "وَإِنْ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلا فِيها نَذِيرٌ" means that every nation among the previous nations had a warner in the past.

It is also noteworthy that according to the above verse, all nations have had a divine warner, i.e., a prophet, although some have taken it in a broader sense to include scholars and sages who warn the people; however, this meaning is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse.

In any case, the meaning of this statement is not that a prophet must be sent to every single city and land; rather, it is sufficient that the call of the prophets and their words reach the populations. This is because the Quran says "خَلا فِيها نَذِيرٌ" (there was a warner in them), and it does not say "minha" (meaning from among themselves).

Therefore, what we read in the above verse does not contradict verse 44 of Surah Saba, which says: "And We had not sent to them before you any warner" (وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْهِمْ قَبْلَكَ مِنْ نَذِيرٌ). Here, the meaning is a warner from among themselves, whereas in the previous verse, the meaning is the arrival of the prophets' call to them.

In the next verse, He adds: "And if they deny you, it is no wonder, and do not be grieved, for those before them also denied [their messengers]. Their messengers came to them with clear proofs, and with the scriptures [containing exhortations], and with the enlightening Book [containing laws and illuminating statutes]" (وَ إِنْ يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَ بِالزُّبُرِ وَ بِالْكِتابِ الْمُنِيرِ).

You are not the only one who, despite having miracles and a heavenly Book, is denied by this ignorant people; the previous prophets also grappled with this problem. Therefore, do not be grieved, step firmly on your path, and know that those who must accept will accept.

Regarding the difference between "Bayyinat" (بينات), "Zubur" (زبر), and "Kitab Munir" (كتاب منير), commentators have expressed various views, the clearest of which are the following two:

1- "Bayyinat" means clear proofs and miracles that establish the truthfulness of the prophet. "Zubur" is the plural of "Zabur," meaning books whose script is written with firmness (like writing on stone and the like), which here is an allusion to the firmness of its contents. [1] In any case, it refers to the books revealed before Moses (عليه السلام), while "Kitab Munir" refers to the Book of Moses and other heavenly books revealed after it (since in the Holy Quran, in Surah Al-Ma'idah verses 44 and 46, the Torah and the Gospel are mentioned as guidance and light, and in verse 15 of the same Surah, the term "light" is also used for the Holy Quran).

2- "Zubur" refers to that part of the prophets' books that contained only advice, exhortation, and supplications (like the Psalms of David). "Kitab Munir" refers to those heavenly books that contain laws, statutes, and various social and individual instructions, such as the Torah, the Gospel, and the Quran. This interpretation seems more appropriate.

In the last verse under discussion, He points to the painful punishment of this group, saying:

[1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Zabartu al-kitab" means I wrote it with great writing, and every book with thick/firm writing is called "Zabur" (Mufradat, root z-b-r).

It was not the case that they would remain immune from divine punishment and continue their denials indefinitely. "Then I seized those who disbelieved, and how [terrible] was My punishment!" (ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا). [1] We afflicted some with the flood, some with a destructive windstorm, and some were crushed by the heavenly cry, lightning, and earthquake! Then, at the end, to emphasize and express the greatness and severity of their punishment, He says:

"How was My punishment [of them]?" (فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكِيرِ).

This is exactly like a person who performs a significant task and then asks those present: "How was my work?"

In any case, these verses, on one hand, provide comfort and encouragement to all wayfarers on the path of Allah, especially the true leaders and guides of every nation in every age and time, so that they do not become discouraged or hopeless by the voices of opposition. They should know that divine calls have always faced intense opposition from stubborn bigots and oppressive profiteers, yet there have also been sincere seekers and devoted lovers who sacrificed their lives alongside the callers to the path of truth.

On the other hand, it is a threat to these stubborn opponents, so they may know that they cannot continue their shameful and destructive actions forever; sooner or later, divine punishments will overtake them.

[1]: "Akhaḏtu" is from the root "akhḏ," meaning to take or seize, but in these instances, it is an allusion to punishment, as seizing and arresting is the prelude to punishment.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ ثَمَرَٰتٍ مُّخْتَلِفًا أَلْوَٰنُهَا ۚ وَمِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ جُدَدٌۢ بِيضٌ وَحُمْرٌ مُّخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَٰنُهَا وَغَرَابِيبُ سُودٌ (٢٧)
Have you not regarded that Allah sends down water from the sky, with which We produce fruits of diverse hues. And in the mountains are stripes, white and red, of diverse hues, and [others] pitch black? (27)
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَٱلدَّوَآبِّ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَٰنُهُۥ كَذَٰلِكَ ۗ إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى ٱللَّهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ ٱلْعُلَمَـٰٓؤُا۟ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ غَفُورٌ (٢٨)
And of humans and beasts and cattle there are likewise diverse hues. Only those of Allah’s servants having knowledge fear Him. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-forgiving. (28)

Commentary

So Many Wondrous Designs Upon the Portal of Existence!

Once again, these verses return to the subject of Monotheism (Tawhid), opening a new page from the Book of Creation before the eyes of humanity to provide a crushing response to the stubborn polytheists and the persistent deniers of Oneness.

On this beautiful page of the great Book of Creation, the diversity of inanimate objects and the various beautiful countenances of life in the realms of plants, animals, and humans are brought to attention—how Allah has brought forth "a hundred thousand colors from colorless water" and created completely diverse beings, each more beautiful than the other, from specific and limited elements.

This masterful Painter, with a single pen and a single ink, has innovated types of designs that captivate, enchant, and fascinate the beholders.

First, He says: "Do you not see that Allah sends down water from the sky, and with it We bring forth fruits of various colors?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَخْرَجْنا بِهِ ثَمَراتٍ مُخْتَلِفاً أَلْوانُها).

The beginning of this sentence with a rhetorical question, while stimulating human curiosity, indicates that this matter is so clear and manifest that anyone who looks will see it. Yes, they see that from uniform water and earth—one colorless and the other possessing only one color—all these different colors of various fruits, beautiful flowers, leaves, and blossoms have emerged in diverse forms.

"Colors" (Alwan): This may refer to the outward colors of the fruits, where even within one type of fruit like the apple, various colors exist, let alone among different fruits. It could also be an allusion to the differences in their taste, structure, and various properties, to the extent that even within one type of fruit, there are various species; for instance, there are perhaps more than 50 types of grapes and about 70 types of dates! It is interesting that in the above verse, it appears in the third person ("Allah sends down..."), then changes to the first person at the start of the next clause, saying: "We bring forth with it fruits of various colors." This style of expression is not unique to this verse; similar instances are found elsewhere in the Holy Quran. It is as if the statement of the first sentence grants the listener a new understanding and recognition of God, and with this recognition, they find themselves present before Allah, who then speaks to them directly.

Following the verse, it points to the diversity of paths found in the mountains, which allows for the distinction of roads from one another, saying: "And in the mountains are tracts, white and red, of varying colors, and (others) intensely black" (وَ مِنَ الْجِبالِ جُدَدٌ بِيضٌ وَ حُمْرٌ مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوانُها وَ غرابِيبُ سُودٌ) [1]. This variation in colors, on one hand, grants a special beauty to the mountains, and on the other, serves as a means for finding paths and not getting lost in the winding mountain roads; ultimately, it is evidence of Allah's power over all things.

"Judad" is the plural of "juddah", meaning a road or path.

"Bid" is the plural of "abyad", meaning white, and "humr" is the plural of "ahmar", meaning red.

"Gharabib" is the plural of "ghirbib", meaning intensely black.

[1]: Some have considered this sentence as an "initial" (istinafiyyah) sentence ("min al-jibal" being the advanced predicate and "judad" the delayed subject), while others say it is implied as: "Do you not see that among the mountains are tracts..."

This is also why the Arabs call the crow "ghurab." Therefore, the mention of the word "sud" (which is also the plural of "aswad", meaning black) immediately after it serves as an emphasis on the intense blackness of some mountain paths [1]. Another possibility suggested in the commentary of the verse is that the mountains themselves are like lines and paths drawn upon the surface of the earth, which are particularly noticeable from a distance—lines that are white, some red, and some intensely black; lines that the hand of the Lord's decree has traced upon the face of the earth [2]. In any case, the structure of the mountains with completely different colors on one hand, and the mountain paths with various hues on the other, is another sign of His greatness, power, and wisdom, which manifests in a different form at every moment and adorns itself in a new garment at every turn.

In the next verse, the issue of the diversity of colors is raised regarding humans and other living creatures, saying: "And of men and moving creatures and cattle, there are likewise those of various colors" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ وَ الدَّوَابِّ وَ الْأَنْعامِ مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوانُهُ‌). Yes, humans, despite all coming from one father and mother, possess completely different races and colors—some white as snow, some black as ink. Even within a single

[1]: As stated by some lexicographers like Lisan al-Arab and some commentators, "sud" in the above verse is a substitute (badal) for "gharabib", because in the case of colors, the emphasis is not placed first (note that "gharabib" carries more emphasis regarding blackness than "sud"); hence they say it was originally "sud gharabib."

[2]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 42.

race, there is much variation in color. In fact, even twin children who have gone through all embryonic stages together and have been in each other's embrace from the start, if we look closely, are not completely identical in color, despite being from one father and mother, conceived at the same time, and nourished by the same type of food.

Beyond the outward appearance, their inner "colors"—their temperaments, characteristics, traits, talents, and tastes—are completely diverse and different, so that together they form a cohesive unit meeting all needs.

In the world of living creatures, there are thousands upon thousands of types of insects, birds, reptiles, sea animals, and wild desert animals, each of which, with its unique characteristics and wonders of creation, is a sign of the Creator's power, greatness, and knowledge.

When we step into a large zoo, even though it contains no more than a fraction of a thousandth of the world's living beings, we become so stunned, bewildered, enchanted, and captivated that we involuntarily open our lips in praise of the Lord who has placed all these wondrous designs upon the portal of existence.

After stating these monotheistic signs, it concludes with a summary: "Even so it is" (kadhalika) [1]. And since the benefit of these great signs of creation is primarily for

[1]: Regarding the grammatical position of "kadhalika", various possibilities have been given. Some consider it an independent sentence where the implication is "The matter is so," and we chose this meaning in the commentary as it is more striking and appropriate. However, some have linked it to the preceding sentence, meaning: "Just as the fruits and mountain tracts are of various colors, so too are humans, moving creatures, and cattle." It is also possible that it relates to the following sentence, meaning: "In like manner, the states of the servants differ in their fear."

wise and knowledgeable servants, the verse continues: "Only those of His servants who possess knowledge fear Allah" (إِنَّما يَخْشَى اللَّهَ مِنْ عِبادِهِ الْعُلَماءُ).

Yes, among all servants, it is the scholars who attain the high station of khashyah—that is, "fear of responsibility coupled with an understanding of the greatness of the Lord's station." This state of khashyah is the product of contemplating the signs in the horizons and within the self, and awareness of the Lord's knowledge, power, and the purpose of creation.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Khashyah is a fear mixed with veneration, and it is mostly used in cases where it stems from knowledge and awareness of something; hence, in the Holy Quran, this station is designated specifically for the learned."

We have repeatedly stated that fear of God means fear of the responsibilities a person has toward Him—fear of falling short in fulfilling one's mission and duty. Furthermore, fundamentally, perceiving greatness—especially a greatness that is unlimited and infinite—is awe-inspiring for a limited being like man (reflect on this).

From this sentence, the conclusion is also well-drawn that true scholars are those who feel a heavy sense of responsibility toward their duties; in other words, they are people of action, not just words. For knowledge without action is evidence of a lack of khashyah, and its possessors are not counted among the "scholars" in the above verse.

This same truth is mentioned in a Hadith from Imam Zayn al-Abidin Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, who said:

"Knowledge of Allah and action are but two inseparable companions. Whoever knows Allah fears Him, and this fear urges him to act in obedience to Allah. The masters of

knowledge and their followers are those who have known Allah well, acted for His sake, and yearned for Him, as Allah has said: 'Only those of His servants who possess knowledge fear Allah'" [1]. In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the commentary of this verse, we read:

"By 'the scholars' is meant those whose actions confirm their words; and whoever's words are not confirmed by their actions is not a scholar" [2]. In another Hadith, it is stated: "The most knowledgeable of you regarding Allah is the one who fears Allah the most" [3]. In short, "scholars" in the logic of the Quran are not those whose brains are small chests for the opinions and thoughts of this person or that, filled with scientific laws and formulas of the world, whose tongues speak of these matters, and whose dwellings are schools, universities, and libraries. Rather, scholars are that group of thinkers and scientists whose light of knowledge has illuminated their entire being with the light of God, faith, and piety, and who feel a profound sense of responsibility toward their duties and are the most committed of all.

In Surah Al-Qasas, we also read that when the arrogant and self-satisfied Qarun, who also claimed a station of knowledge, displayed his wealth, the crowd of world-seekers—who were deeply impressed by that glitter—wished they too had such a share of worldly goods. But the "scholars" of the Children of Israel shouted at them: "Woe to you! The reward of Allah is better for those who believe and do righteous deeds, and none shall attain it except the patient and steadfast": (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَيْلَكُمْ ثَوابُ اللَّهِ خَيْرٌ لِمَنْ آمَنَ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً وَ لا يُلَقَّاها إِلَّا الصَّابِرُونَ) (Al-Qasas: 80).

At the end of the verse, as a brief reason for what preceded, He says: "Indeed, Allah is Almighty, All-Forgiving" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ غَفُورٌ).

His "Might" (Izzah) and infinite power are the source of the fear and khashyah of the thinkers, and His "Forgiveness" (Ghafuriyyah), which is a sign of His endless mercy, is the cause of their hope. Thus, these two holy names keep the servants of God between fear and hope, and we know that continuous movement toward perfection is not possible without being characterized by these two attributes.

[1]: Rawdat al-Kafi, as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 359.

[2] and [3]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَتْلُونَ كِتَـٰبَ ٱللَّهِ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً يَرْجُونَ تِجَـٰرَةً لَّن تَبُورَ (٢٩)
Indeed those who recite the Book of Allah and maintain the prayer, and spend secretly and openly out of what We have provided them, expect a commerce that will never go bankrupt, (29)
لِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدَهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ (٣٠)
so that He may pay them their full reward and enhance them out of His bounty. Indeed He is all-forgiving, all-appreciative. (30)

Commentary

A Highly Profitable Transaction with the Lord!

Since the previous verses referred to the status of "fear and awe" (khashyat) of the scholars, the verses under discussion point to their status of "hope and expectation." As we have stated, it is only by means of these two wings that a human being can fly to the heights of the heaven of felicity and traverse the path of perfection. First, He says: "Indeed, those who recite the Book of Allah, and establish prayer, and spend out of what We have provided them, secretly and openly, hope for a commerce that will never perish" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَتْلُونَ كِتابَ اللَّهِ وَ أَقامُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ أَنْفَقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقْناهُمْ سِرًّا وَ عَلانِيَةً يَرْجُونَ تِجارَةً لَنْ تَبُورَ) [1].

It is evident that "recitation" here does not mean a superficial reading devoid of reflection and thought; rather, it is a reading that serves as a source of thought—a thought that becomes the source of righteous deeds. These are deeds that, on one hand, connect man to God, manifested in prayer, and on the other hand, connect him to God's creation, manifested in spending (infaq). This spending encompasses everything Allah has given to man: from his knowledge, his wealth and influence, his powerful intellect, to his ethics and experiences—in short, from all God-given blessings.

This spending sometimes takes place secretly to be a sign of complete sincerity (sirran). And sometimes it is done openly to encourage others and to exalt the symbols of religion (ʿalaniyah). Indeed, the knowledge that produces such an effect is a source of hope and expectation.

Based on what has appeared in this verse and the preceding one, we conclude that true scholars possess these characteristics: - Spiritually, their hearts are filled with awe and fear mingled with the greatness of Allah. - In speech, their tongues are occupied with the recitation of Allah's verses. - In spiritual and physical action, they perform prayer and worship Him. - In financial action, they spend from what they have, both secretly and openly. - Finally, in terms of their goal, their intellectual horizon is so high that they have detached their hearts from the fleeting material world, looking only toward the highly profitable Divine commerce, which the hand of annihilation cannot reach.

It is also noteworthy that "tabur" is derived from "bawar," meaning extreme stagnation (of trade). Since extreme stagnation leads to ruin, "bawar" has come to mean destruction. Thus, "a commerce free from bawar" is a trade that suffers from neither stagnation nor corruption!

In a fascinating hadith, it is narrated: "A man said to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: 'Why do I not love death?' He asked: 'Do you have any wealth?' The man replied: 'Yes.' The Prophet said: 'Send it ahead of yourself.' The man said: 'I cannot!' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'The heart of a man is with his wealth; if he sends it ahead, he loves to join it, and if he keeps it behind, he loves to remain with it!'" [1]

This hadith, in fact, reflects the spirit of the above verse, for it says that those who establish prayer and spend in the way of Allah have hope and affection for the next abode, because they have sent their good deeds ahead of themselves and desire to join them.

The final verse under discussion describes the goal of these true believers as follows: "That He may give them their rewards in full and increase them out of His grace. Indeed, He is All-Forgiving, All-Appreciative" (لِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَ يَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ إِنَّهُ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ) [1].

This phrase actually points to the ultimate sincerity of their purpose—that in their righteous deeds, they look to nothing but the Divine reward. Whatever they want, they seek from Him, and they do not take a single step for the sake of hypocrisy, showing off, or the praise and adulation of others; for the most important issue in righteous deeds is a pure intention.

The use of the term "ujur" (plural of ajr), meaning "wages," is in fact a grace from the Lord. It is as if He considers the servants to be His creditors in exchange for their righteous deeds, even though everything the servants possess is from Him—even the power to perform righteous deeds was granted by Him.

Even more affectionate than this expression is the phrase "and increase them out of His grace," which gives them the glad tidings that, in addition to the ordinary reward—which itself is sometimes hundreds or thousands of times the deed—He will add to them from His grace. He bestows upon them blessings that no mind can fathom and no one in this world has the ability to imagine, from His vast bounty.

In a hadith from Ibn Mas'ud, it is mentioned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said in the interpretation of this very verse: "It refers to the station of intercession (shafa'ah) given to them, so they may intercede for those who did them a kindness in the world but deserve punishment due to their actions." [2] Thus, they are not only saved themselves but, by the grace of the Lord, are a means of salvation for others.

Some commentators have considered the phrase "and increase them out of His grace" as a reference to the station of "witnessing" (shuhud) that occurs for the believers on the Day of Resurrection, where they gaze upon the Beauty and Majesty of the Lord and derive the highest pleasure from this vision. However, it appears that the mentioned phrase has a broad meaning that includes both the content of the hadith and other unknown blessings.

The phrase "Indeed, He is All-Forgiving, All-Appreciative" shows that the first grace of the Lord toward them is the forgiveness of sins and lapses that may have occurred, as man's greatest concern is from this direction. Once they are at peace in this regard, He includes them in His "appreciation" (shukr), meaning He appreciates their deeds and grants them the most excellent reward.

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, an interesting proverb is quoted from the Arabs: "More appreciative than the Baruqah tree." This refers to a small tree in the Arabian Peninsula. The belief of the Arabs was that when a cloud casts a shadow over it, it quickly turns green and puts forth leaves, even without the cloud raining! This is a proverb for ultimate gratitude—giving the greatest reward for the smallest service. [2] Certainly, the Creator of such a tree is even more appreciative and generous than that.

Key Point: The Extraordinary Conditions of This Commerce

It is interesting that in many verses of the Quran, this world is likened to a marketplace where the traders are human beings, the Customer is the Great Lord, the merchandise is righteous deeds, and the price is Paradise, His mercy, and His pleasure. [1] If we reflect deeply, this extraordinary commerce with the Generous Lord is unique, for it possesses advantages found in no other trade:

  1. He Himself has placed the entire capital at the disposal of the seller, then He comes forward to purchase it!
  2. He is the Buyer, while He has no need to purchase these deeds, for the treasuries of all things are with Him!
  3. He buys "meager goods" for a "massive price": "O He who accepts the little and pardons the much."
  4. He is a buyer of even the most insignificant goods: "So whoever does an atom's weight of good shall see it."
  5. Sometimes He gives the price seven hundredfold, and sometimes even more than that (Baqarah: 261).
  6. In addition to paying this immense price, He adds to it from His grace and mercy that which cannot be imagined: "and increase them out of His grace" (the verse under discussion).

How regrettable it is for a free, rational human being to close his eyes to such commerce and turn to something else—and worse yet, to sell the essence of his existence for nothing!

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says: "Indeed, there is no price for your souls except Paradise, so do not sell them for anything else." [1]
