Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِعِبَادِهِۦ لَخَبِيرٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (٣١)
That which We have revealed to you of the Book is the truth, confirming what was [revealed] before it. Indeed Allah is all-aware, all-seeing about His servants. (31)
ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱصْطَفَيْنَا مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ۖ فَمِنْهُمْ ظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مُّقْتَصِدٌ وَمِنْهُمْ سَابِقٌۢ بِٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَضْلُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (٣٢)
Then We made those whom We chose from Our servants heirs to the Book. Yet some of them are those who wrong themselves, and some of them are average, and some of them are those who take the lead in all the good works by Allah’s will. That is the greatest grace [of Allah]! (32)

Commentary

The True Heirs to the Legacy of the Prophets

Since the preceding verses spoke of the pure-hearted believers who recite and act upon the verses of the Divine Book, the verses under discussion speak of this Heavenly Book, the evidences of its truthfulness, and its actual bearers. It completes the discussion regarding Monotheism (Tawhid) in the previous verses with this discussion centered on Prophethood.

It states: "And that which We have revealed to you of the Book is the truth, confirming what was before it. Indeed, Allah is All-Aware and All-Seeing of His servants" (وَ الَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ مِنَ الْكِتابِ هُوَ الْحَقُّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِعِبادِهِ لَخَبِيرٌ بَصِيرٌ).

Considering that "Truth" (al-Haqq) refers to that which is consistent and harmonious with reality, this expression serves as a proof to establish that this Heavenly Book has been sent down by the Lord. For the more we scrutinize its content, the more we find it in harmony with realities.

There is no contradiction in it; no lies or superstitions are found within it. Its beliefs and teachings are consistent with the logic of reason, its histories are free from myths and fables, and its laws are in accord with human needs. This truthfulness is a clear proof that it has been revealed by Allah.

Here, the word "Truth" (Haqq) is used to explain the status of the Quran, while in other verses, words such as "Light" (Nur), "Proof" (Burhan), "Criterion" (Furqan), "Reminder" (Zikr), "Admonition" (Maw'izah), and "Guidance" (Huda) are used—each referring to one of the blessings and dimensions of the Quran. The word "Haqq" encompasses all of them.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: The root of "Haqq" means correspondence and agreement, and this word is applied to several meanings:

First: One who creates something based on wisdom; for this reason, Allah is called "The Truth" (al-Haqq): فَذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمُ الْحَقُّ "For that is Allah, your Lord, the Truth" (Yunus: 32).

Second: Something created based on wisdom is also called "Haqq." Since the universe is the act of God and accords with wisdom, all of it is "Haqq," as the Quran says: ما خَلَقَ اللَّهُ ذلِكَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ "Allah did not create these (the sun, the moon, and their stations) except in truth" (Yunus: 5).

Third: Beliefs that correspond to realities are called "Haqq": فَهَدَى اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الْحَقِّ "Then Allah guided those who believed to the truth concerning that over which they had differed" (Al-Baqarah: 213).

Fourth: Words and actions performed according to duty and at the appointed time are also called "Haqq," just as we say: "Your word is truth and your deed is truth." [1] Therefore, the Quran being "Haqq" is from the perspective that it is a word consistent with interests and realities, and that the beliefs and knowledge within it harmonize with reality. It is also the work of God who created it based on wisdom, and Allah Himself—who is the essence of Truth—has manifested within it, and reason confirms that which is truth and reality.

The phrase "confirming what was before it" (مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ) is another proof of the truthfulness of this Heavenly Book, as it aligns with the signs mentioned in previous scriptures regarding it and its bringer (we have provided a detailed discussion on this under verse 41 of Surah Al-Baqarah). [2] The phrase "Indeed, Allah is All-Aware and All-Seeing of His servants" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ بِعِبادِهِ لَخَبِيرٌ بَصِيرٌ) explains the reason for the Quran's truthfulness and its harmony with realities and needs, for it was revealed by the God who knows His servants well and is All-Seeing regarding their needs.

Regarding the difference between "Khabir" (All-Aware) and "Basir" (All-Seeing), some have said "Khabir" refers to knowledge of the inner depths, beliefs, intentions, and the spiritual structure of man, while "Basir" refers to seeing his outward appearance and physical phenomena. [1] Others consider "Khabir" an allusion to the origin of man's creation and "Basir" an allusion to his deeds and actions. [2] Of course, the first interpretation seems more appropriate, though it is not unlikely that both meanings are intended by the verse.

[1] Al-Mufradat by Raghib, under the root "Haqq." [2] Refer to Volume 1, page 210 onwards. [1] Fakhr al-Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir under the verse. [2] Ruh al-Bayan under the verse.

The next verse addresses an important subject in this regard: the bearers of this great Heavenly Book—those who, after the revelation of the Quran upon the pure heart of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), preserved and guarded this blazing torch during that time and through subsequent centuries and ages. It says: "Then We caused to inherit the Book those We have chosen of Our servants" (ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا الْكِتابَ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنا مِنْ عِبادِنا).

It is clear that "the Book" here refers to the same thing mentioned in the previous verse (the Holy Quran), and the definite article "Al-" (the) is for specific reference (Al-Ahd).

The suggestion by some that it refers to all Heavenly Books—taking the "Al-" as generic—seems very unlikely and does not harmonize with the preceding verses.

The use of the term "inheritance" (irth) here, and in other similar instances in the Quran, is because "inheritance" refers to something obtained without trade or toil, and Allah placed this immensely great Book at the disposal of the Muslims in this manner.

Here, numerous narrations have been transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) in which "the chosen servants of Allah" are interpreted as the Infallible Imams (peace be upon them). [3]

[3] Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, page 361 onwards.

As we have stated many times, these narrations identify the clearest and primary instances (masadiq), and they do not prevent the scholars of the Ummah, the righteous, and the martyrs—who strove to guard this Heavenly Book and perpetuate its commands—from being included under the title "those We have chosen of Our servants" (الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنا مِنْ عِبادِنا).

Then, it proceeds to an important classification in this regard, saying:

"Among them is he who wrongs himself, and among them is he who is moderate, and among them is he who is foremost in good deeds by Allah's permission. That is the great favor" (فَمِنْهُمْ ظالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ وَ مِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ وَ مِنْهُمْ سابِقٌ بِالْخَيْراتِ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْكَبِيرُ).

The apparent meaning of the verse is that these three groups are among the "chosen of Allah" who are the heirs and bearers of the Divine Book.

To put it more clearly: Allah entrusted the guardianship of this Heavenly Book after His Prophet to this Ummah—an Ummah chosen by Allah. However, within this Ummah, different groups are found: some fall short in their great duty of guarding this Book and acting upon its rulings, and in reality, they wrong themselves; these are the instances of "he who wrongs himself" (zalimun li-nafsihi).

Another group has risen to this duty of guardianship and acting upon the Book to a great extent, although they have had slips and inadequacies in their work; these are the instances of "the moderate" (muqtasid).

And finally, an elite group has performed their heavy duties in the best possible manner and has surpassed everyone else in this great field of competition; this leading group is the one referred to in the above verse as "foremost in good deeds by Allah's permission" (سابِقٌ بِالْخَيْراتِ بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ).

It might be asked here: how can the existence of the "wrongdoer" (zalim) group be compatible with the phrase "We have chosen" (astafayna), which implies that all these groups are Allah's chosen ones?

In response, we say this is similar to what is mentioned regarding the Children of Israel in verse 53 of Surah Ghafir: "And We certainly gave Moses guidance, and We caused the Children of Israel to inherit the Book" (وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْهُدى‌ وَ أَوْرَثْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ). This is while we know that not all of the Children of Israel fulfilled their duty toward this great inheritance.

Or it is like verse 110 of Surah Al-Imran which says: "You are the best nation produced [as an example] for mankind" (كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ).

Or in verse 16 of Surah Al-Jathiyah regarding the Children of Israel: "And We preferred them over the worlds" (وَ فَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ).

Similarly, in verse 26 of Surah Al-Hadid, we read: "And We already sent Noah and Abraham and placed in their descendants prophethood and scripture; and among them is he who is guided, but many of them are defiantly disobedient" (وَ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً وَ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ جَعَلْنا فِي ذُرِّيَّتِهِمَا النُّبُوَّةَ وَ الْكِتابَ فَمِنْهُمْ مُهْتَدٍ وَ كَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ فاسِقُونَ).

In short, the aim of such expressions is not every single individual of the Ummah, but rather the Ummah as a whole, even if different strata and groups are found among them. [1]

[1] As for the possibility suggested by some that this division refers to "Our servants" (ibadina) rather than the "chosen" among the servants—meaning these three groups are not found among the heirs of the Divine Book but rather among the general body of Allah's servants, and that the "chosen" are only the third group (the foremost in good deeds)—this seems very unlikely. The apparent meaning is that these groups are from those being discussed in the verse, and we know the verse is not talking about all servants, but about the chosen ones. Furthermore, the annexation of "servants" to "Our" (ibadina) conveys a type of praise and honor that is inconsistent with the second interpretation.

In many narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), the "foremost in good deeds" is interpreted as the Infallible Imam, the "wrongdoer to himself" as those who lack recognition (ma'rifah) of the Imam, and the "moderate" as the knowledgeable followers of the Imam (peace be upon him). [1] These interpretations are clear evidence for what we chose in the commentary of the entire verse: that there is no obstacle to these three groups existing among the heirs of the Divine Book.

It perhaps goes without saying that the interpretation in the above narrations is of the type of identifying the clearest instances; that is, the Infallible Imam is in the first rank of the "foremost in good deeds," while scholars, thinkers, and guardians of the Divine religion occupy other ranks.

The interpretation regarding the "wrongdoer" and the "moderate" in these narrations is also of the type of identifying instances.

And if we see in some narrations that the inclusion of scholars in the concept of the verse is entirely denied, it is in fact to draw attention to the presence of the Infallible Imam at the forefront of these ranks.

It is noteworthy that a group of past and contemporary commentators have offered many other possibilities in interpreting these three groups, all of which are in fact identifying instances. [2]

[1] Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, page 361 onwards, and also Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, Chapter: "Indeed, those whom Allah has chosen from His servants..." [2] Some have said the "foremost in good deeds" are the companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), the "moderate" are the generation of the "Followers" (Tabi'un), and the "wrongdoers to themselves" are others! Others have interpreted the "foremost" as those whose inner state is better than their outer, the "moderate" as those whose outer and inner are the same, and the "wrongdoer" as those whose outer is better than their inner! Some said the "foremost" are the Companions, the "moderate" are their Followers, and the "wrongdoers" are the hypocrites. Some considered the verse an allusion to the three groups mentioned in Surah Al-Waqi'ah, verses 7 to 11: "And you become [of] three kinds: Then the companions of the right - what are the companions of the right? And the companions of the left - what are the companions of the left? And the foremost, the foremost - those are the ones brought near [to Allah]." In a hadith, the "foremost in good deeds" is interpreted as the noble Imams: Ali (peace be upon him), Imam Hasan, Imam Husayn, and the martyrs of the Family of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family); the "moderate" as the striving religious people; and the "wrongdoer" as those who have mixed their righteous deeds with unrighteous ones. All these interpretations are acceptable as identifying instances, except the first one which lacks a sound concept.

A question arises here: why does He first mention the group of wrongdoers, then the moderate, and then the foremost in good deeds, while from some perspectives, the reverse seems more appropriate?

Some great commentators have answered this by saying the goal is to state the order of people's stations in the evolutionary chain. The first stage is the stage of disobedience and negligence; after that is the station of repentance and turning back (inabah); and finally, the station of attention and proximity to God. When a sin is committed by a person, he is a "wrongdoer"; when he returns to the station of repentance, he is "moderate"; and when his repentance reaches the station of acceptance and his struggles in the way of Allah increase, he reaches the station of proximity and is placed in the chain of the "foremost in good deeds." [1] Some have added that this order is due to the abundance or scarcity of individuals in these three groups: the wrongdoers form the majority, the moderate are in the next stage, and the foremost in good deeds—who are the elite and the pure—are the fewest, even if they are the highest in terms of quality. [1] Interestingly, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), it is narrated that he said: He put the wrongdoer first so that he would not despair of His mercy, and He put the foremost in good deeds last so that they would not become conceited by their deeds. [2] All three meanings may be intended.

The final word in the commentary of this verse is that regarding the phrase "That is the great favor" (ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْكَبِيرُ), there is discussion among commentators as to what "that" refers to.

Some have said it is the inheritance of the Divine Book itself, and some have considered it an allusion to the success (tawfiq) that encompasses the foremost in good deeds, who traverse this path by Allah's permission. However, the first meaning is more consistent with the apparent structure of the verse.

Key Points Who are the Guardians of the Divine Book?

By the testimony of the Holy Quran, the Great God has given immense gifts to the Islamic Ummah, the most important of which is this great Divine legacy, the Quran.

He chose the Muslim Ummah over other nations and gave them this blessing, but in the same proportion that He has shown them His special grace, He has also placed a heavy responsibility upon them.

They can only fulfill the right of guarding this immense legacy if they join the ranks of the "foremost in good deeds."

That is, they must surpass all nations in performing good deeds; they must take the lead in acquiring knowledge and science; they must be pioneers in piety and God-wariness, in worship and service to creation, in Jihad and effort, in order and accountability, and in altruism and sacrifice. In all these matters, they must be the vanguard; otherwise, they have not fulfilled its right.

Specifically, the expression "foremost in good deeds" has such a vast and expansive meaning that it includes being ahead in all positive aspects of life and righteous actions.

Yes, the bearers of such a legacy can only be such people.

Even those who turn their backs on this great Heavenly gift and do not respect its sanctity wrong themselves, according to the instance of "wrongdoer to himself," because its content is nothing but their salvation, happiness, and victory. He who casts a healing prescription behind him has contributed to the continuation of his own pain and suffering, and he who breaks his bright lamp while traversing a dark path drives himself toward deviation and the abyss, for Allah is Self-Sufficient and independent of all.

At the same time, this group of sinners must not forget the fact that they too, according to the content of the above verse, were among the "chosen of the Lord" and potentially have the talent to leave the stage of "wrongdoing" behind, step into the stage of the "moderate," and from there fly to the peak of the glory of the "foremost in good deeds," for they too are the chosen of the Truth in terms of their primordial nature (fitrah) and spiritual structure.

[1] Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan under the verse. [1] Fi Zilal al-Quran under the verses. [2] Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 9, under the verses.

Ayahs

جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيهَا مِنْ أَسَاوِرَ مِن ذَهَبٍ وَلُؤْلُؤًا ۖ وَلِبَاسُهُمْ فِيهَا حَرِيرٌ (٣٣)
Gardens of Eden, which they will enter, adorned therein with bracelets of gold and pearl, and their garments therein will be of silk. (33)
وَقَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَذْهَبَ عَنَّا ٱلْحَزَنَ ۖ إِنَّ رَبَّنَا لَغَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ (٣٤)
They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has removed all grief from us. Indeed Our Lord is all-forgiving, all-appreciative, (34)
ٱلَّذِىٓ أَحَلَّنَا دَارَ ٱلْمُقَامَةِ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ لَا يَمَسُّنَا فِيهَا نَصَبٌ وَلَا يَمَسُّنَا فِيهَا لُغُوبٌ (٣٥)
who has settled us in the everlasting abode by His grace. In it we are untouched by toil, and untouched by fatigue.’ (35)

Commentary

Where There Is Neither Grief, Nor Toil, Nor Exhaustion!

These verses are, in fact, the result of what was mentioned in the previous verses. It says: "The reward for those who were foremost in good deeds is the Gardens of Eternity, which they shall all enter" (جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَها) [1]. "Jannat" is the plural of "Jannah," meaning garden, and "Adn" means stability and permanence. A mine is called "Ma'dan" because it is the place where metals and jewels are established and remain. Therefore, "Jannat Adn" refers to the eternal gardens of Paradise.

In any case, this expression indicates that the great blessings of Paradise are eternal and constant, and unlike the favors of the material world, they are not mixed with the anxiety arising from the fear of cessation. The inhabitants of Paradise do not possess just one garden, but rather many gardens at their disposal.

Then, it points to three categories of Heavenly blessings—some of which are material and outward, some spiritual and inward, and a part which pertains to the negation and removal of any obstacle or disturbance—stating:

"These foremost in good deeds are adorned in those eternal Gardens with bracelets of gold and pearls, and their garments therein are silk!" (يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيها مِنْ أَساوِرَ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ وَ لُؤْلُؤاً وَ لِباسُهُمْ فِيها حَرِيرٌ).

In this world, they were indifferent to glitz and glamour and did not make themselves captives of ornaments. While the deprived did not even have canvas clothing to wear, they were not preoccupied with luxurious garments. To compensate for this, Allah clothes them in the other world with the finest garments and ornaments.

In this world, they adorned their inner selves with good deeds; thus, in the other world—which is the world of the embodiment of actions—Allah adorns their outward appearance with various types of jewelry.

We have said many times: our words, which were established for the limited life of this world, can never fully express the concepts of the great realm of the Resurrection. To describe those blessings, another alphabet and a different culture and lexicon are required. However, in order to show a shadow of those great blessings to us, the prisoners of this world, we must seek help from the meager capacity of these very words to explain those favors.

After mentioning this material blessing, He points to a specific spiritual blessing, saying:

"And they say: Praise be to Allah, who has removed from us all grief" (وَ قالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَذْهَبَ عَنَّا الْحَزَنَ).

They praise and glorify Allah for this great favor bestowed upon them, whereby all factors of grief and sorrow have been removed from their living environment by the grace of Divine favor, and the sky of their souls has been cleared of the dark clouds of sadness. They have no fear of Divine punishment, no dread of death or annihilation, no causes for mental insecurity, and no harassment from the ill-intentioned, nor impositions from the wicked and tyrants, nor the companionship of the evil and the unworthy.

Some commentators have considered this "grief and sorrow" to refer to sorrows similar to those in this world, while others consider it a reference to the anxiety they have in the Gathering Place (Mahshar) regarding the outcome of their deeds. However, these two interpretations are not in conflict and can be combined in the meaning of the verse.

"Hazan" (rhyming with 'adam') and "Huzn" (rhyming with 'muzd'), as stated in many books of linguistics and commentary, both carry the same meaning. Originally, it means the unevenness of the ground, and since grief and sorrow make the human soul uneven and rough, this expression is used in this sense [1]. Then these believers in Paradise add: "Indeed, our Lord is Oft-Forgiving, Most Appreciative" (إِنَّ رَبَّنا لَغَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ).

With His attribute of Forgiveness (Ghafuriyyah), He has removed the heavy sorrow of lapses and sins, and with His attribute of Appreciation (Shakuriyyah), He has granted us eternal favors upon which the ominous shadow of grief never falls.

His forgiveness has covered our many sins, and His appreciation has granted abundant rewards for our few and meager deeds.

Finally, it turns to the last blessing, which is the absence of the side effects of discomfort and the factors of hardship, fatigue, toil, and weariness. Quoting them, it says: "Praise be to that God who, out of His grace, has settled us in the Home of Eternal Settlement, where neither toil touches us nor weariness affects us!" (الَّذِي أَحَلَّنا دارَ الْمُقامَةِ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ لا يَمَسُّنا فِيها نَصَبٌ وَ لا يَمَسُّنا فِيها لُغُوبٌ).

On one hand, it is the "Home of Settlement," and it is not such that as soon as a person wants to become familiar with its environment and grow attached to it, the call of "departure" is sounded.

On the other hand, although its long duration joins eternity—and during such a period, one would normally expect some fatigue, pain, or hardship—there is absolutely no sign of these things there. Even the length of time does not cause boredom or exhaustion, for every day a new blessing and a fresh manifestation of the Lord's favors and glories are presented to the inhabitants of Paradise.

"Nasab" (rhyming with 'hasab') means hardship and labor. Many linguists and commentators have considered "Lughub" to have the same meaning, while some have distinguished between the two as follows: "Nasab" refers to physical hardships, and "Lughub" refers to spiritual toil and trouble [1]. Some have also defined "Lughub" as the weakness and exhaustion resulting from hardship and pain; thus, "Lughub" becomes the result of "Nasab" [2]. Accordingly, there are neither physical factors of hardship there, nor any news of the causes of spiritual suffering.

[1] (Footnote from the original text regarding the grammatical parsing of "Jannat Adn"). [1] (Footnote from the original text regarding the linguistic roots of "Hazan" and "Huzn"). [1] and [2] Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 22, p. 184, under the verse being discussed.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَهُمْ نَارُ جَهَنَّمَ لَا يُقْضَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ فَيَمُوتُوا۟ وَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُم مِّنْ عَذَابِهَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى كُلَّ كَفُورٍ (٣٦)
As for the faithless, there is for them the fire of hell: they will neither be done away with so that they may die, nor shall its punishment be lightened for them. Thus do We requite every ingrate. (36)
وَهُمْ يَصْطَرِخُونَ فِيهَا رَبَّنَآ أَخْرِجْنَا نَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا غَيْرَ ٱلَّذِى كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ ۚ أَوَلَمْ نُعَمِّرْكُم مَّا يَتَذَكَّرُ فِيهِ مَن تَذَكَّرَ وَجَآءَكُمُ ٱلنَّذِيرُ ۖ فَذُوقُوا۟ فَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِن نَّصِيرٍ (٣٧)
They shall cry therein for help: ‘Our Lord! Bring us out, so that we may act righteously—differently from what we used to do!’ ‘Did We not give you a lifelong enough that one who is heedful might take admonition? And [moreover] the warner had [also] come to you. Now taste [the consequence of your deeds], for the wrongdoers have no helper.’ (37)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَـٰلِمُ غَيْبِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٣٨)
Indeed Allah is the knower of the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. Indeed, He knows well what is in the breasts. (38)

Commentary

Return Us So That We May Act Righteously!

Usually, the Quran presents "promises" alongside "threats" and "glad tidings" alongside "warnings" to strengthen the two factors of fear and hope, which are the motivations for evolutionary movement. This is because man, by virtue of self-love, is influenced by the instincts of "seeking benefit" and "repelling harm." Therefore, following the previous verses which spoke of the great and soul-nourishing rewards for the "believers who hasten toward good deeds," the verses under discussion speak of the painful punishment of the disbelievers.

Here, too, the discussion concerns both material and spiritual punishments.

First, It says: "And those who disbelieve, for them is the fire of Hell" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَهُمْ نارُ جَهَنَّمَ‌).

Just as Paradise is a permanent station and an eternal abode, Hell is also the eternal dwelling place for this group.

Then It adds: "Death is never decreed for them so that they might die" and be released from this suffering and pain (لا يُقْضى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ فَيَمُوتُوا) [1]. Even though that scorching fire and all those painful torments could swallow them into the jaws of death at any moment, since the command of Allah—to whom everything belongs, including life and death—has not been issued, they do not die. They must remain alive to taste the divine punishment.

[^1]: "La yuqda 'alayhim" means "it is not decreed upon them."

Death for such individuals would be a gateway to salvation, but with the preceding sentence, this gateway is closed. There remains another possibility: that they stay alive but their punishment is gradually lightened, or their endurance is increased so that the result is a reduction in pain and suffering. He closes this gateway as well with another phrase, saying: "nor shall its punishment be lightened for them" (وَ لا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمْ مِنْ عَذابِها).

At the end of the verse, as an emphasis on the decisiveness of this divine threat, He says: "Thus do We requite every ungrateful disbeliever!" (كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي كُلَّ كَفُورٍ).

Those who, in the first instance, were ungrateful for the blessing of the existence of prophets and heavenly books, and then squandered the God-given capitals that could have helped them attain felicity. Yes, the reward of the ungrateful is burning in the painful torment of fire—a fire they ignited with their own hands in the life of this world, and whose fuel consists of their thoughts, actions, and very existence.

Since "Kafur" (کفور) is an intensive form (Sighat al-Mubalaghah), it carries a deeper meaning than "Kafir" (disbeliever). Furthermore, the word "Kafir" is used in contrast to "Mu'min" (believer), but "Kafur" is used regarding the ingratitude for all blessings; thus, its concept is broader. In this way, "Kafur" refers to those who have been ungrateful for all divine blessings and have closed all doors of His mercy upon themselves in this world; therefore, in the Hereafter, Allah also closes all doors of salvation to them.

The next verse addresses another part of their painful punishment and points to some sensitive matters in this regard, saying: "And they will cry out therein: 'Our Lord, bring us out; we will act righteously, other than what we used to do!'" (وَ هُمْ يَصْطَرِخُونَ فِيها رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا نَعْمَلْ صالِحاً غَيْرَ الَّذِي كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ‌) [1]. Yes, upon witnessing the results of their evil deeds, they fall into deep remorse and cry out from the depths of their hearts, making an impossible request: the request to return to the world to perform righteous deeds.

The use of "Salihan" (صالحاً - in the indefinite form) suggests that "we did not perform even the slightest righteous deed," and its implication is that all this torment and suffering is for those who had no path toward God in life and were drowned in rebellion and sin. Therefore, performing even some righteous deeds might be a cause for salvation.

The expression "Na'mal" (نعمل), which is a present/future tense verb indicating continuity, is also an emphasis on this meaning: that we were constantly engaged in unrighteous deeds.

Some commentators have said: Describing "righteous [deeds]" with the phrase "other than what we used to do" contains a subtle point, which is that "we used to perceive our ugly deeds as righteous deeds due to the beautification of the carnal soul and Satan; now we intend to return and perform truly righteous deeds, different from what we had."

Indeed, at the beginning, a sinner perceives the ugliness of his actions according to pure human nature, but gradually he becomes accustomed to them, and their vileness diminishes in his eyes. Gradually, he goes beyond this, and they appear good to him, as the Quran says: "(The evil of) their deeds has been made fair-seeming to them" (زُيِّنَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ أَعْمالِهِمْ‌) (At-Tawbah: 37).

And sometimes It says: "And they think that they are doing good work" (وَ هُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعاً) (Al-Kahf: 104).

[^1]: "Yastarikhoon" is from the root "Surakh," meaning a loud cry that a person lets out from the heart in a state of seeking help and calling for an assistant to remove pain and suffering.

In any case, a decisive response is given to them by Allah regarding this request; He says: "Did We not give you life long enough for whoever would take heed to take heed therein?" (أَ وَ لَمْ نُعَمِّرْكُمْ ما يَتَذَكَّرُ فِيهِ مَنْ تَذَكَّرَ).

"And did the warner not come to you?" (وَ جاءَكُمُ النَّذِيرُ).

Now that this is the case, and all means of salvation were at your disposal and you did not utilize them, you must remain trapped here: "So taste [the punishment], for there is no helper for the wrongdoers!" (فَذُوقُوا فَما لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ نَصِيرٍ).

This verse explicitly states: You lacked nothing, for you had sufficient opportunity at your disposal, and the divine warner came to you in the necessary measure, and these two pillars of awakening and salvation were achieved. Therefore, there is no excuse or pretext for you. If you did not have sufficient respite, there would be an excuse; and if you had respite but no teacher, mentor, leader, or guide came to you, there would again be an excuse. But with the existence of these two, what excuse or pretext remains?! "Nadhir" (the warner) usually refers in Quranic verses to the existence of the prophets, especially the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. However, some commentators have mentioned a broader meaning here that includes the prophets, the heavenly books, and awakening events such as the death of friends and relatives, and old age and weakness—especially since in Arabic poetry, the word "Nadhir" is frequently used to mean old age, such as the following verse: "I saw gray hair as one of the warners of death for its owner, and sufficient is it as a warner for you!" [1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

It is also noteworthy that in Islamic narrations, various expressions have been recorded regarding the limit of age that is sufficient for a person's awakening and reflection:

In some, it is interpreted as sixty years, as we read in a hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "Whomever Allah grants sixty years of life, He has left him no excuse." [1] The same meaning has been narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام. [2] In another hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "When the Day of Resurrection comes, a caller will cry out: 'Where are the sixty-year-olds?' And that is the age about which Allah said: 'Did We not give you life long enough for whoever would take heed to take heed therein?'" [3] However, in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, the amount is set at only eighteen years. [4] Of course, it is possible that the latter narration refers to the minimum, and the previous narrations refer to the maximum; therefore, there is no contradiction between these narrations. It can even be applied to other ages—depending on the individuals—and in any case, the breadth of the verse's concept remains preserved.

In the last verse under discussion, He responds to the request the disbelievers have in Hell to return to the world: "Indeed, Allah knows the unseen of the heavens and the earth; such a God..."

[^1] & [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [^3]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi and Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur. [^4]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed.

"...God certainly knows what is within the hearts" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عالِمُ غَيْبِ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

In reality, the first sentence is a proof for the second sentence; that is, how is it possible for Allah to be unaware of the secrets within the hearts while all the secrets of the earth and the heavens and the unseen of the universe are manifest to Him?

Yes, He knows that if the request of the people of Hell were answered affirmatively and they returned to the world, they would continue the same past actions, just as it is explicitly stated in verse 28 of Surah Al-An'am: "And if they were returned, they would certainly revert to that which they were forbidden, and indeed they are liars" (وَ لَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ‌).

In addition, the verse is a warning to all believers to strive for sincerity in their intentions and to have no one in mind but Allah; for if there is the slightest impurity in their intention and motivation, He, who is aware of all hidden things, knows it and will requite accordingly.

Key Points

1- What is meant by "Dhat al-Sudur"?

In many verses of the Holy Quran (more than 10 verses), this phrase is repeated exactly or with a slight variation: "Indeed, Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

The word "Dhat" (ذات), whose masculine form is "Dhu" (ذو), originally means "owner" or "possessor," although in the terminology of philosophers it is used to mean the essence, reality, and core of things. However, according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, this is a terminology that does not exist in the [original] Arabic language.

Therefore, the phrase "Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts" means that Allah is aware of the "owner" and "master" of the hearts. This phrase is a subtle metaphor for the beliefs and intentions of humans, because when beliefs and intentions become established in the heart, they seemingly become the masters of the human heart and rule over it. For this reason, these beliefs and intentions are considered the owner and master of the human heart.

This is what some great scholars have utilized and embodied in this expression: "Man is his opinions and thoughts, not his form and limbs!" [1]

2- There is no way back!

Certainly, the Resurrection and life after death are an evolutionary stage relative to the world, and returning from it to this world is not rational. Can we return to yesterday? Can a newborn return to the embryonic period? And is it possible for a fruit that has been separated from the branch to return to the branch? For this reason, returning to the world is not possible for the people of the Hereafter.

Furthermore, even assuming such a return were possible, the forgetful human would surely continue in his previous ways! Let us not look far; we have tested ourselves many times—in specific conditions where we are in a bind, we make sincere pacts with our God, but as soon as those conditions change, we completely forget the promises and pacts. Except for those who truly undergo a deep transformation, not a transformation conditional on those same circumstances which reverts to the original state when they change.

[^1]: The noble scholar, the late Kashif al-Ghita in Asl al-Shi'a wa Usuluha.

This reality is mentioned in several verses of the Holy Quran, including verse 28 of Surah Al-An'am mentioned above, where the Quran explicitly refutes such people, saying: "If they were returned, the program would be the same as the former program."

However, in verse 53 of Al-A'raf, It contents itself with saying that they are losers, but does not give an explicit answer to their request for return: "Are there any intercessors for us who might intercede for us? Or could we be sent back so that we might do other than what we used to do? They have lost their souls, and that which they used to invent has failed them" (فَهَلْ لَنا مِنْ شُفَعاءَ فَيَشْفَعُوا لَنا أَوْ نُرَدُّ فَنَعْمَلَ غَيْرَ الَّذِي كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ قَدْ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ‌).

The same meaning appears in verses 107 and 108 of Surah Al-Mu'minun in another way: "Our Lord, bring us out of it; then if we return [to evil], we will indeed be wrongdoers. He will say: 'Remain despised therein and do not speak to Me!'" (رَبَّنا أَخْرِجْنا مِنْها فَإِنْ عُدْنا فَإِنَّا ظالِمُونَ قالَ اخْسَؤُا فِيها وَ لا تُكَلِّمُونِ‌).

In any case, these are futile requests and impossible wishes, and perhaps they themselves know this more or less, but out of the intensity of desperation and helplessness, they repeat this request. We must do whatever we want [to achieve] while we still have the opportunity today.

Ayahs

It is He who made you successors on the earth. So whoever is faithless, his unfaith is to his own detriment. The unfaith of the faithless does not increase them with their Lord [in anything] except disfavour, and their unfaith increases the faithless in nothing except loss. (39)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ شُرَكَآءَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ أَمْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ كِتَـٰبًا فَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَتٍ مِّنْهُ ۚ بَلْ إِن يَعِدُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ بَعْضُهُم بَعْضًا إِلَّا غُرُورًا (٤٠)

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُمْسِكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ أَن تَزُولَا ۚ وَلَئِن زَالَتَآ إِنْ أَمْسَكَهُمَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ حَلِيمًا غَفُورًا (٤١)
Indeed Allah sustains the heavens and the earth lest they should fall apart, and if they were to fall apart, there is none who can sustain them except Him. Indeed He is all-forbearing, all-forgiving. (41)

Commentary

The Heavens and the Earth Stand by the Hand of His Power!

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding the fate of the

Ayahs

They had sworn by Allah with solemn oaths that if a warner were to come to them, they would be better guided than any of the nations. But when a warner came to them, it only increased their distance [from the truth], (42)

due to their domineering [conduct] in the land and their devising of evil schemes; and evil schemes beset only their authors. So do they await anything except the precedent of the ancients? Yet you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent, and you will never find any revision in Allah’s precedent. (43)
أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ وَكَانُوٓا۟ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً ۚ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعْجِزَهُۥ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَلِيمًا قَدِيرًا (٤٤)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentaries of Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ruh al-Ma'ani, Mafatih al-Ghayb, and other exegeses, it is mentioned that when the Arab polytheists heard that some of the previous nations, such as the Jews, had denied the Divine Prophets and martyred them, they would say: "But we are not like that! If a Divine messenger comes to us, we will be the most guided of all nations!" However, when the world-illuminating sun of Islam rose from the horizon of their land, and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله came to them with the greatest Heavenly Book, they not only refused to accept it but rose up in denial, combat, and various forms of trickery and deception.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 18; p. 292

The above verses were revealed and rebuked them for these hollow and baseless claims. [^1]

[^1]: Most commentaries under the verses in question.

Commentary

Arrogance and Deception Were the Source of Their Misery

In the preceding verses, the discussion concerned the polytheists and their fate in this world and the Hereafter; the verses under discussion continue this same subject.

The first verse says: "And they swore by Allah their most binding oaths that if a warner came to them, they would surely be more guided than any [other] of the nations" (وَ أَقْسَمُوا بِاللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لَئِن جاءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ لَيَكُونُنَّ أَهْدى‌ مِنْ إِحْدَى الْأُمَمِ). [^1] Ayman is the plural of yamin, meaning an oath. Originally, yamin means the right hand, but since people would give their right hands to one another when swearing an oath or making a covenant, this word gradually came to be used for the oath itself.

Jahd is from the root jihad, meaning effort and exertion; therefore, the expression jahda aymanihim refers to an emphasized and solemn oath.

Indeed, when they observed the pages of past history, which spoke of the infidelities, ingratitude, obstructions, and crimes of previous nations—especially the Jews—against their Prophets, they were greatly surprised and made all sorts of claims about themselves, boasting loudly.

[^1]: Since ahad (one) is singular, the literal meaning of the verse at first glance would be that they would be more guided than "one" of the nations, which likely refers to the Jewish people (as a singular in an affirmative sentence does not denote generality). However, as some commentators have noted, contextual clues indicate that by this singular form, they meant generality, for they were in a state of exaggeration and emphasis, wanting to claim that if a Prophet were sent among them, they would surpass all nations.

But when "the touchstone of experience arrived" and the furnace of trial grew intensely hot and their wish was fulfilled, they showed that they were of the same cloth. As the Quran states in the continuation of this verse: "But when a warner came to them, it did not increase them except in aversion" (فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ ما زادَهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُوراً).

This expression shows that even before this, contrary to their claims, they were not supporters of the truth. They did not respect the parts of the Abrahamic religion that existed among them, and they trampled it underfoot every day on some pretext. They placed no value or worth on the "independent dictates" of reason and the commands of the intellect. With the rise of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and the wounding of their ignorant prejudice, and the endangering of their illegitimate interests, their distance from the truth increased. Yes, they were always distant from the truth, and now more than ever.

The next verse is an explanation of what passed in the previous verse. It says: "Their distance from the truth was because they took the path of arrogance in the land, and were never willing to submit to the truth" (اسْتِكْباراً فِي الْأَرْضِ) [^1] "and also because they practiced evil plotting" (وَ مَكْرَ السَّيِّئِ). [^2]

[^1]: Many commentators have said that istikbaran is grammatically the "object of reason" (maf'ul lahu), explaining the cause of their nufur (aversion) and distance from the truth, and they consider makra as-sayyi' to be coordinated with it, while some consider it coordinated with nufuran.

[^2]: Makra as-sayyi' is of the type of adding the genus to the species, like ilm al-fiqh, because makr means any kind of planning or devising, whether good or bad; hence it is sometimes attributed to Allah: وَ مَكَرُوا وَ مَكَرَ اللَّهُ (Surah Al-Imran 54). However, sayyi' is a specific type of makr, which is malicious plotting.

"But the evil plot encompasses only its authors" (وَ لا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ).

The phrase la yahiq is from the root haq, meaning "it does not descend," "it does not strike," and "it does not encompass." This indicates that plots may temporarily affect others, but ultimately they return to the plotter, disgracing and impoverishing him before God's creation and shaming him in the Divine presence—the same grim fate that befell the polytheists of Mecca.

In reality, this verse says: they were not content merely with distancing themselves from this great Divine Prophet, but they used all their power and strength to strike at him, and the primary motivation for this was pride, arrogance, and a lack of humility before the truth.

In the continuation of the verse, He threatens this arrogant, plotting, and treacherous group with a meaningful and soul-shaking sentence, saying: "Then do they await except the way of the former peoples?" (فَهَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا سُنَّتَ الْأَوَّلِينَ). [^1] This short sentence is an allusion to all the grim fates of the neck-strong and rebellious nations such as the people of Noah, 'Ad, Thamud, and the people of Pharaoh, each of whom was afflicted by a great calamity. The Quran has repeatedly mentioned glimpses of their grim and painful fate, and here, with this single short sentence, it visualizes all of them before the eyes of this group.

Then, for further emphasis, He adds: "But you will never find in the way of Allah any change, and you will never find in the way of Allah any alteration" (فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا وَ لَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللَّهِ تَحْوِيلًا).

[^1]: Nazar and intizar (waiting/expecting), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, sometimes carry the same meaning.

How is it possible for Allah to punish a nation and a population for certain deeds but exempt another group that follows the same program? Is He not Wise and Just? Does He not perform everything based on wisdom and justice?

Fluctuation and alteration of "Sunnah" (ways/laws) are conceivable for someone who has limited knowledge and, with the passage of time, becomes aware of issues that deter him from his old ways, or someone who is aware but does not act according to the scales of wisdom and justice, being governed by specific desires. But the Lord, who is sanctified and pure from all such things, His Sunnah regarding future generations is the same as it was for the predecessors; His laws are fixed and immutable.

The Quran emphasizes the immutability of Divine Sunnahs in numerous verses, which we discussed in detail under verse 62 of Surah Al-Ahzab (Volume 17).

In brief, in this world, in the realms of creation and legislation, there are fixed and unchangeable laws that the Quran refers to as Divine Sunnahs, which never undergo alteration. Just as these laws governed the past, they govern today and tomorrow. The punishment of faithless arrogant ones when Divine advice yields no benefit, as well as the aiding of those who walk the path of truth when they do not cease their sincere efforts, are among these Sunnahs; both have been and remain immutable in the past and present. [^1] It is noteworthy that in some verses of the Holy Quran, only the lack of tabdil (replacement) of Divine Sunnahs is mentioned (Al-Ahzab: 62), and in others, the lack of tahwil (alteration) (Surah Al-Isra: 77).

[^1]: In this regard, in addition to Volume 17 under verse 22 of Surah Al-Ahzab, we also discussed this in Volume 12 under verse 77 of Surah Al-Isra (Volume 12, page 218).

But in the verse under discussion, He brings both consecutively for emphasis, saying: "You will find neither tabdil nor tahwil for the Sunnah of Allah!" Do these both convey the same meaning for emphasis, or does each refer to an independent meaning?

Considering the roots of these two words, it appears they refer to two different meanings: tabdil is to replace something entirely—that is, to remove it and put something else in its place—whereas tahwil is to change that same existing thing in terms of "quality" or "quantity."

In this way, Divine Sunnahs are neither replaced entirely, nor are they even increased, decreased, weakened, or intensified. This includes the fact that Allah ordains similar punishments for similar sins and crimes in every respect; He does not ordain a punishment for one group and exempt another, nor does He make the punishment of one group less or weaker. Such is a law derived from a fixed root that contains neither replacement nor alteration or change. [^1] The final point regarding this verse is that in one place "Sunnah" is attributed to "Allah," and in another part of the same verse, "Sunnah" is attributed to "the former peoples" (al-awwalin). At first glance, a contradiction might be imagined between these two, but it is not so.

[^1]: A group of commentators has interpreted tahwil here to mean "transferring the place of punishment," meaning that Allah removes His punishment from one person and places it on another. While this interpretation does not seem appropriate for the above verse—the discussion is not about punishing someone in place of another, but rather that the punishment does not increase, decrease, change, or replace—it seems these commentators have confused the root tahawwul with tahwil. In some linguistic texts like Majma' al-Bahrayn, it is stated: At-Tahwil: making a thing contrary to what it was; and At-Tahawwul: moving from one place to another!

In the first instance, the attribution is to the Agent (the Lawgiver), and in the second instance, it is to the object (those upon whom the law is applied). In the first case, the talk is of the Establisher of the Sunnah, and in the second, it is of those regarding whom this Divine Sunnah is executed.

The next verse invites this group of polytheists and criminals to follow the traces of the predecessors and the fate they suffered, so that they may see with their own eyes in the lands belonging to them and among their ruins what they have heard from history, so that report may be transformed into vision.

He says: "Have they not traveled through the land and observed how was the end of those before them?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ).

If they imagine they are stronger than them, they are gravely mistaken, for "they were superior to them in power" (وَ كانُوا أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً).

The Pharaohs, who made the land of Egypt the arena of their power, and the Nimrods, who ruled with total power over the expanse of Babylon and other lands, were so strong that the idolaters of Mecca are of no account compared to them.

Furthermore, no matter how powerful and strong humans may be, their power is zero compared to the power of Allah, for "Allah is not such that anything in the heavens or in the earth should escape Him [or frustrate Him]. Indeed, He is ever Knowing and Competent" (وَ ما كانَ اللَّهُ لِيُعْجِزَهُ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ لا فِي الْأَرْضِ). [^1]

[^1]: The phrase li-yu'jizahu, as we have said before, is from the root i'jaz, meaning to make someone unable; for this reason, in many cases, it means escaping the realm of power or not being able to be apprehended.

He is both All-Knowing and All-Powerful; nothing remains hidden or concealed from His sight, no task is difficult for His power, and no one can overcome Him.

If these arrogant blind-hearted ones and malicious plotters think they can escape the grasp of His power, they are sadly mistaken; and if they do not cease their ugly and shameful deeds, they will ultimately suffer the same deadly fate as the previous tyrants.

Repeatedly in the Holy Quran, we encounter this point where Allah invites faithless and rebellious individuals to "travel through the land" and observe the traces of nations that were afflicted by Divine punishment.

In Surah Ar-Rum, verse 9, it is stated: "Have they not traveled through the earth and observed how was the end of those before them? They were greater than them in power, and they plowed the earth and built it up more than they [the Meccans] have built it up, and their messengers came to them with clear proofs. And Allah would never have wronged them, but they were wronging themselves."

Similar meanings appear in Surah Yusuf, verse 109; Surah Al-Hajj, verse 46; Surah Ghafir, verses 21 and 82; and Surah Al-An'am, verse 11, as well as some other chapters of the Quran.

These repeated emphases are evidence of the extraordinary effect of these observations on human souls. They must go and see with their eyes what they have read in history or heard from people.

They should go and see the plundered glory of the Pharaohs, the ruined palaces of the Khosrows, the crumbled graves of the Caesars, the decayed and dusted bones of the Nimrods, and the disaster-stricken lands of the people of Lot and Thamud. They should hear the advice of the silent ones, listen to the roar of those sleeping in the heart of the earth, and see with their own eyes what will eventually happen to themselves!

I went to Egypt and saw the ancient ruins; In Egypt, I saw what I had heard in stories. I had read much of this and that in history; In Egypt—to be honest—I saw it with my own eyes. You saw a palace, and I saw those sleeping in the heart of the earth; I saw them still seeking an eternal kingdom. You saw a crown, and I saw a kingdom gone to plunder; You saw ivory, and I saw a handful of bones. You saw a throne, and I saw fortune overturned from the throne; You saw a rock, and I saw the mockery of time. What the past held hidden in the heart of the future; In Egypt—to be honest—I saw it with my own eyes. [^1]

[^1]: One of the contemporary poets has composed interesting verses in this regard, bringing this Quranic truth into his soul-shaking poems with very delicate and attractive expressions after traveling to Egypt and observing the ruins of the Pharaohs. He says: [The poem translated above]. Regarding "traveling through the land" and studying the creative signs of God as well as the traces of predecessors and its extraordinary effects on building the human spirit, we had a detailed discussion under verse 137 of Surah Al-Imran (Volume 3, page 102).

Ayah

وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّاسَ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ مَا تَرَكَ عَلَىٰ ظَهْرِهَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِۦ بَصِيرًۢا (٤٥)
Were Allah to take humans to task because of what they have earned, He would not leave any living being on its back. But He respites them until a specified time, and when their time comes, [He retributes them in accordance with their works], for Allah has been watching His servants. (45)

Commentary

If Not for His Grace, No Creature Would Remain on the Face of the Earth!

The above verse, which is the final verse of Surah Fatir, concludes the intense discussions and severe warnings of this Surah by expressing the Grace and Mercy of the Lord toward the people of the earth, just as it began the Surah with the opening of Allah’s Mercy upon mankind.

In this way, its beginning and end are united and harmonious in expressing Divine Mercy.

Furthermore, the preceding verse, which threatened the faithless criminals with the fate of those before them, raises a question for many of them and others: if the Divine Sunnah (way) regarding all tyrants is such, then why does He not punish this polytheistic and rebellious tribe of Mecca?! In response to this question, He says: "And if Allah were to punish people for what they have earned (and give them no respite for reform, reconsideration, and self-improvement), He would not leave a moving creature on its back [i.e., the earth]" (وَ لَوْ يُؤاخِذُ اللَّهُ النَّاسَ بِما كَسَبُوا ما تَرَكَ عَلى‌ ظَهْرِها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ).

Successive punishments would descend, and lightning bolts, earthquakes, and storms would crush the oppressive sinners such that the earth would no longer be a place for anyone to live.

"But He grants them respite until a specified term, and gives them the opportunity for repentance and reform" (وَ لكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى‌).

However, this Divine forbearance and opportunity have a limit; it lasts until their appointed term arrives. "But when their term comes, He will recompense everyone according to their deeds, for Allah is ever All-Seeing of His servants." He sees their actions and is aware of their intentions (فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ بِعِبادِهِ بَصِيراً). [^1] Here, two questions arise, the answers to which become clear from what we have discussed:

First: Does this general ruling ("If Allah were to punish people for their deeds, He would leave no one on the face of the earth") also include the Prophets, the Awliya (Saints), and the righteous?!

[^1]: The phrase "فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ" (But when their term comes) is a conditional clause, and its response is implied. In reality, it was: "When their term comes, He recompenses everyone for what they did." Therefore, the phrase "فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ" (for Allah...) serves as the reason for the recompense, replacing the omitted effect. There is also the possibility that the response is "they cannot delay it for an hour, nor can they advance it," as mentioned in other verses of the Quran like verse 61 of Surah An-Nahl. Thus, the phrase "فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كانَ بِعِبادِهِ بَصِيراً" indicates that He knows everyone and knows whose term has arrived so that He may seize them with His power.

The answer to this question is clear, because such rulings refer to the masses of humanity and their overwhelming majority, and the Prophets, Imams, and the righteous—who are in the minority—are certainly excluded from it. In short, every rule has an exception, and they are exceptions to this rule.

This is exactly like when we say the people of the world are negligent, greedy, and arrogant, meaning the majority of them. In verse 41 of Surah Ar-Rum, we read: "Corruption has appeared throughout the land and sea by [reason of] what the hands of people have earned so He may let them taste part of [the consequence of] what they have done that perhaps they will return [to righteousness]." It is obvious that corruption is not the result of the actions of all people, but rather refers to the majority.

Verse 32 of this same Surah, which divides humans into three groups: "the wrongdoer," "the moderate," and "the foremost in good deeds" (the virtuous and pure), is another witness to this meaning.

Therefore, the above verse in no way contradicts the issue of the infallibility (Ismah) of the Prophets.

Second: Does the term "dabbah" (moving creature) in the above verse include non-humans as well, meaning they too would perish because of the punishment of humans?! The answer to this question is clear when considering that the philosophy of the existence of other creatures is for humans to benefit from them; when the human race is removed, there is no necessity for their existence. [^1]

[^1]: "Dabbah" is derived from the root "dabib," meaning to walk slowly and take short steps, but linguistically it usually includes every moving creature, whether it moves fast or slow. However, sometimes "dawab" is specifically applied to riding animals.

Finally, we conclude this discussion with a Hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the interpretation of this final verse:

According to this Hadith, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has said: "O son of Adam! It is by My will and desire that you were created with free will, and you can desire for yourself whatever you wish. And by My will, you became the possessor of will, able to intend for yourself whatever you want. With the blessings I gave you, you gained strength and committed disobedience against Me, and with My power and health, you were able to perform My obligations. Therefore, I am more worthy of your good deeds than you are, and you are more worthy of your sins than I am. Goodness continuously reaches you from Me through the blessings I have given you, and evil and badness continuously reach you from yourself because of your crimes... I never neglected to warn and advise you, and I did not punish you immediately at the time of your arrogance and negligence" (rather, I placed sufficient opportunity for repentance and reform at your disposal). Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: This is what Allah says: "And if Allah were to punish people for what they have earned, He would not leave a moving creature on its back." [^1]

O Allah! Make us among those who wake up before opportunities are lost and return to You, and who illuminate their dark past with the light of good deeds and by seeking Your pleasure. O Lord! If Your Mercy did not encompass us, the fire flaring up from within our evil deeds would have swallowed us, and if the light and radiance of Your forgiveness were not sprinkled upon our hearts, the army of Satan would have occupied it.

O Allah! Keep us away from every kind of polytheism, and kindle the lamp of faith and pure monotheism in our hearts, and increase the light of Taqwa (piety) in our speech and actions.

End of Surah Fatir 12/ Rajab/ 1404
